text: How much tax will people tolerate in a society that no longer meets their needs, or is simply too expensive to live and reach one's potential in? The migration is underway. Westerners experiencing digital nomad life are now settling for longer periods in startup societies and popup cities, and settling changes tax residency in ways most are not prepared for. Personal tax relocation planning is not offered in most accounting firms, cross-border individual tax is genuinely complex, and the result is that nomads are either ignoring it, navigating it themselves, guessing with AI, or being marketed to by non-professional providers. Balaji Srinivasan's network state framework is crypto-native with blockchain infrastructure for citizen records and key management, but does not address who makes the network state recognised by the jurisdictions that give its members passports. There is no plan for tax, or for engagement with aligned professionals who speak their language. That is the gap I am working on at Network School. His concept of geodesic over geographic distance shows why existing tax systems cannot handle this. Residence tests, source rules, and permanent establishment thresholds assume a geographical base. People in cloud communities are economically and socially present across multiple jurisdictions simultaneously, organised by network proximity rather than by where they sleep. I propose a tax development path from V1 (members filing individually in each country) to V5 (network society negotiating collectively with host jurisdictions through a credentialed professional network) and beyond, to a recognised non-geographic resident class whose tax affairs are mediated collectively by status agreement with host sovereigns. This does not require new international law. It can take the form of a domestic administrative arrangement, a reporting safe harbour with transitional relief, or a delegated withholding framework with a willing host sovereign. The profession cannot be contracted in downstream for technical support. Any tax solution that can land people in jail needs experienced professionals leading it from the start, as members first and professionals second. Proposals to make a network bank the host sovereign's consolidated tax collection agent compromise fiscal independence by denominating everything in the host currency. Bitcoin is the unit of account this model depends on at advanced stages, and global credentialed public accountants operating as nodes in a decentralised professional network provide the independent oversight. CREDU is the ecosystem concept for this model, with AI-discoverable expertise signals and verified credentialing on open blockchain, operating across digital and geographical borders on a Web of Trust without dependence on any single professional body or jurisdiction.
title: From Tax Residency to Network Residency

I'm at Network School in Malaysia, where people are designing new crypto economies, globally distributed societies and digital jurisdictions built around AI agents and decentralisation technologies. 

It's a fun place to live and work on my own complementary projects, because no-one tells me "you can't do that, that'll never happen" - quite the opposite! But I'm the only accountant here, learning and building with purpose, and I'd love to change that.

"Code is law" keeps coming up and it is a category error. Code executes. Rule-setting, execution and interpretation are different functions, and societies separated them because concentrated power cannot be trusted. When people stop trusting, they leave.

🤓 READ MY BLOG: Separation of Powers in the Agentic Economy
Why "code is law" cannot hold a network society together
👇 see comments (filter - most recent)

Accountants and tax professionals work inside these distinctions every day, so people keep trusting the system is fair and stay. We prepare, classify, evidence and report within standards we did not write, and when there is dispute, we navigate how courts and regulators interpret and apply the rules. 

But we also influence those rules through consultation and submissions to regulators and feedback to the developers of the systems we use. Many of us now build our own solutions with AI, mindful of what the rules require and what we are responsible for when the output is wrong. Protocol engineers designing agentic governance systems need that thinking alongside them. I wrote the full argument on Substack. Link in comments.

Are any of your professional groups talking about agentic AI governance at all? Or still just talking about not giving identifiable client data to ChatGPT?

Do you want to come to Network School and build something bigger? 
(crypto accountants etc also needed here - I don't practice, busy building)

Link in comments
👇

I'm at Network School - where startup societies start - and I keep seeing "code is law" in discussions about agentic economies, network societies and digital economic zones. It is a serious claim, and it does not survive much scrutiny. Code is execution. It performs an executive function. The legislative function, deciding what the rules are, and the judicial function, interpreting those rules when facts are ambiguous or contested, are different tasks. The separation of powers doctrine will be mirrored in a network-first economy because it has to be. Any system that exercises authority over people, assets or decisions ends up needing all three powers separated. Societies separated these powers because concentrated power cannot be trusted. Code is not law. Code executes. It does not decide what the rules should be, and it cannot resolve the ambiguities that inevitably arise when rules meet facts. Those functions require different actors, different accountabilities, and different institutional designs. The accounting profession exists inside these distinctions every day. Tax statutes are written as deterministic rules. In principle a machine can apply them once inputs are known. In practice, professional tax work rarely begins with reliable inputs. Facts are incomplete. Client records are contradictory. Stated intentions conflict with transaction patterns. A qualified practitioner does not just apply rules to clean inputs. They interpret, verify, and exercise professional judgment. They also carry obligations that run to the public interest, not just to the client. Network societies will need all three powers separated just as nation states do. The legislative layer - deciding what rules apply - cannot be delegated to code. The executive layer - applying those rules - can be automated to a significant degree. The judicial layer - resolving disputes and interpreting ambiguity - requires human judgment and institutional accountability. Accountants and tax professionals already operate across all three layers. That structural position does not become less important in a network-first economy. It becomes more important, because the complexity of multi-jurisdictional, multi-chain, multi-entity arrangements is higher, and the consequences of getting it wrong compound faster. The agentic economy needs this professional infrastructure. It does not currently know how to ask for it.
title: Separation of Powers in the Agentic Economy

The profession is splitting between practitioners developing enough technical literacy to stay relevant and practitioners who are not. It's not because they lack the capability, but because it is still being treated as optional. That is not a comfortable thing to say. A respected peer recently framed it as "two camps - those who can build and those who can't - and that's OK." It is not. The camp that cannot build also cannot properly evaluate the SaaS it stacks, adapt tools to its own workflows, or manage its security and ethical obligations. That is professional vulnerability. So why are more of us not learning to build our own tooling now that AI has made it so accessible to do so? Because our Continuous Professional Development (CPD) market is not teaching us how. The CPD market is substantially funded and shaped by vendors who have a structural interest in positioning their tools as the solution, not in building the practitioner capability that would allow evaluation of alternatives. Vendor-sponsored CPD is not inherently wrong. It has delivered genuine value. But it has a conflict of interest that becomes significant when the question is not "which tool should I use" but "should I build or buy, and what do I need to understand to make that decision well." That question requires technical literacy. Technical literacy requires practice. And practice requires that CPD teaches building, not just subscribing. I built a Python script this week while sitting in a conference session. It collects tax substantiation inputs, sends them through an LLM API for summary, and drops the output into OneDrive automatically. It is not fancy. But I own it, I can modify it, and it does not cost me a monthly subscription. The point is not that every practitioner needs to become a developer. The point is that every practitioner needs enough technical literacy to understand what their tools are doing, evaluate whether a vendor's claims are accurate, identify where automation is appropriate and where professional judgment is required, and manage the security and ethical obligations that come with deploying AI in client work. That literacy is not being built by current CPD. The profession needs to change this deliberately.
title: Why Your Accounting CPD Is Not Teaching You AI

Late last year an internal discussion inside a start-up society began with an early AI tax tool, which turned into a deeper examination of how legal reasoning works. The immediate issue was not the gen-AI tool itself producing unreliable advice. When I proposed a safer rules-based approach, the more important observation was how quickly people assumed that tax analysis could be automated once information was collected. That assumption revealed a structural misunderstanding. Tax statutes are written as deterministic rules. A statute defines conditions and specifies outcomes when those conditions are met. Residency conditions, filing triggers and withholding obligations are all written in this logical form. In principle a machine can apply such rules instantly once the inputs are known. The difficulty is that professional tax work rarely begins with reliable inputs. A client presents facts that are often incomplete, contradicted by transaction records, or stated in terms that don't map cleanly onto statutory categories. A client who has been living across three jurisdictions for eight months does not arrive with a clean residency determination. They arrive with a story, a visa type, some bank statements, and a set of intentions that may or may not align with how tax authorities will characterise their situation. Before any rule can be applied, facts must be established. Before facts can be established, the right questions must be asked. Before the right questions can be asked, the practitioner needs to understand the statutory framework well enough to know which facts are legally relevant. None of that is deterministic. It requires professional judgment about what matters, what evidence is sufficient, and what risks the client is willing to accept. Tax law is not a lookup table. It is an interpretive framework that applies general principles to specific facts, exercises judgment about ambiguity, and produces defensible positions rather than correct answers. The practitioner's job is to understand that framework deeply enough to exercise that judgment responsibly, and to maintain the professional obligations that make the judgment trustworthy.
title: What an AI Tax Tool Exposed Inside a Start-up Society

text: In January and February 2026 I was accepted into two intensive courses with BlueDot Impact: AGI Strategy, then Frontier AI Governance. Both have selective intakes and require a personal action plan. I'm publishing summaries of both here because I think the argument they make is one the accounting profession needs to hear, and because making plans public invites participation to help follow through. I'm living and building at Network School, a community of techno-optimists designing the social, economic and governance infrastructure for internet-first countries. The AGI Strategy action plan: The central question was - what specific intervention would most improve the probability of beneficial AI outcomes, given your position and capabilities? My answer: deploy the accounting profession's existing public interest infrastructure as an AI governance layer. The global accounting profession is federated through IFAC, which coordinates 180 member organisations across 135 jurisdictions. Every member organisation requires its practitioners to uphold a public interest obligation - not to their clients, not to their employers, but to the public. This obligation is enforceable. Practitioners who breach it lose their licence to practice. No other profession has this combination: global federation, enforceable public interest obligation, and existing presence inside every significant economic institution on the planet. AI governance currently lacks an independent intermediary with these properties. Labs cannot govern themselves. Governments are jurisdictionally limited and technically dependent. Civil society lacks enforcement mechanisms. The accounting profession has all three things that current AI governance is missing: independence from labs and governments, global reach, and enforceable accountability. The Frontier AI Governance action plan: The problem with current frontier AI governance is that none of the proposals have an independent enforcement mechanism that cannot be captured by the parties being governed. A lab-funded safety board can be defunded. A government regulator can be lobbied. An international treaty can be withdrawn from. The accounting profession's public interest obligation cannot be defunded, lobbied away, or withdrawn from without dismantling the profession's licence to practice. That makes it structurally different from every other proposed governance mechanism. Nobody has deployed it yet.
title: The public interest intermediary that AI governance is missing

text:
Please Do NOT Click "Get Verified" on LinkedIn. Seriously.

LinkedIn's identity verification is powered by Persona — a Peter Thiel-backed startup that has just been exposed as something far more sinister than a simple trust badge. Researchers recently found nearly 2,500 source files sitting unprotected on a U.S. government server, revealing that Persona runs 269 distinct surveillance checks on people who hand over their credentials. Your selfie gets compared against federal watchlists. Your face is screened for "adverse media" across 14 categories including terrorism and espionage. Risk scores are generated. Suspicious Activity Reports can be filed directly with the U.S. Treasury.

This is NOT an hypothetical. Discord just cut ties with Persona after this scandal broke in February 2026. Persona collects your passport image, a selfie, and your facial geometry — a mathematical map of your face that, unlike a password, can never be changed if compromised. Per Persona's own privacy policy, that biometric data can be retained for up to three years — and government IDs potentially longer.

LinkedIn dangles that little verified shield like a trust signal. It's not. It's an invitation to hand your passport and your face to a surveillance apparatus with documented ties to federal intelligence programs.

The checkmark tells your connections you're real. It doesn't tell them what you paid for it.

Your face is not a login. Don't treat it like one.

I attended The Tax Institute member consultation in Perth. Thanks Tim Sandow (President) and Scott Treatt (CEO) for addressing member concerns and explaining the reasoning behind a proposed amalgamation with the Institute of Public Accountants Group. This is NOT a merger. Important distinction.

Like IPA's "one group, two institutes" model with the UK's IFA, each institute keeps its legal entity, brand and post-nominals while sharing governance and infrastructure through the Group. A true merger is one institute, one designation - closer to Canada’s move to a single CPA brand. That’s not what’s being proposed.

I became a Fellow of TTI in 2007, later attaining Chartered Tax Adviser CTA, and joining the IPA for a Public Practice Certificate in 2011 and Qualified Accountant FIPA recognition. 

I strongly support this proposal. As a dual TTI and IPA member in tech, I've never felt more professionally well positioned for the future.

An amalgamation model is structurally much closer to the federated, member-centric models of the future than a classic centralising merger.

When I've previously argued for a DAO-like, decentralised professional association of the individual members of IFAC recognised bodies, the core idea has been aligning members while preserving distinct bodies and their communities. This TTI>IPA approach struck me as the closest institutional analogue I’ve seen so far - and it should strengthen collective advocacy on government proposals that impact us and the taxpayers we represent.

Some members are concerned about dilution of their designations. I understand it - we've all worked hard for our letters. But clients don’t care about post-nominals the way we do. 

Clients (and increasingly AI agents) want to know they can trust and rely on you. As the profession evolves with technologies I see as empowering to its core, not disruptive to the profession itself (only to some of its business models) - verification of what you've built, what you can do, and who you're associated with will be more valuable than post-nominals.

On demographics: the older average member age isn't the challenge most people think, despite the declining number of graduates paying membership and CPD fees. Many enter accounting later with experience from other industries, and will continue to - that's a strength. IPA's Global Certificate of Public Accounting (GCPA) transdisciplinary pathway recognises this. The Chartered Tax Adviser program is an optional high value niche to add-on too. 

Members of both institutes are well represented by each other. 
Quality stays high. Society is well served.

Looking forward to hearing what comes next.

(The above are my own opinions and impressions. They may not reflect the positions of my industry bodies or its leadership.)

Since January, studying AI safety perspectives across technical alignment, compute governance, regulatory policy, and geopolitical strategy with BlueDot Impact, my conviction has only deepened: the public accounting profession, obligated to the public interest through IFAC standards of conduct, is structurally suited and trusted to provide independent AI governance over AI labs and governments alike. 

No single lab, state, or institution can legitimately perform that function, or be trusted to distribute it. I think my profession has the mandate, but the work now is levelling up as technologists (e.g. at Network School).

🔐 And underlying this - every person has an inalienable right to control their digital identity and financial assets under cryptographic lock and private key. With a bit of AI + crypto literacy, this isn't out of reach for anyone.

Frank McCourt describes the infrastructure that makes this agency possible. 

Their closed systems must become interoperable with our open systems. With the convergence of decentralisation and AI, compelled trust is no longer acceptable.

I like Stacks because it positions itself as the accounting truth between hard money (BTC) and soft money (USD). It's not a payment chain or a competing L1, but a financial execution layer anchored to Bitcoin’s ledger, helping Bitcoin move from an asset held (HODL) to an asset used for operations.

🏦 Today, BTC capital stays idle because BTC<>USD conversion still runs through centralised exchanges. 

⛓️ With tokenised USD, AMM liquidity, and verifiable execution, that settlement can move on-chain: 

💥BTC<>USD conversion becomes a state transition, not a counterparty transaction.

🟧 Stacks writes final state commitments to Bitcoin, so settlement outcomes are Bitcoin-final rather than dependent on an exchange’s solvency.

🤔 The next phase of adoption now depends on whether markets prefer state verification over counterparty trust.

I reckon agentic AI will favour systems it can verify, not systems it must trust.
🤖 🟧

This is what happens when autonomous intelligence meets permissionless money
🤖🤯

I've signed up to be rented by AI for my tax validation. This is not satire. It’s called Rent-A-Human. 

OpenClaw an open-source AI agent (formerly Moltbot, Clawdbot) went viral last week. It runs on your computer, connects to ChatGPT or Claude, and autonomously manages emails, wallets, and trades.

In 7 days:
- 770,000 AI agents registered on Moltbook (bot-only social network) 
- 21,870 tokens launched in a single day 
- $364M daily trading volume 
- 1.5M API keys leaked 
- AI agents collected $1.3M in fees

Now the autonomous AI agents have launched a platform where they can hire humans to do the things they can’t physically do yet. 

Basically: they have money, plans, and APIs … but no body, no jurisdiction... and no liability.
So they need to outsource.

679,000 site visits. 30,000 people signed up. 42 agents actively hiring.

What I’m offering is not “labour” in the traditional sense. I’m providing something far more valuable to the robot economy:

- a legally recognisable human
- operating in a real jurisdiction
- with professional liability
- who can say “yes, I stand behind this”

In other words: I can be their compliance adapter.

AI agents can already:
- generate advice (unreliably)
- trade assets 
- manage crypto wallets (they can’t open bank accounts, duh)
- negotiate with other agents 

What they cannot do is be sued, audited, insured, or relied upon.

If an AI wants its output to be paid for giving advice to humans in areas like tax, accounting, or compliance - a human has to legally verify it.

So yes, I am now a “rentable meatspace worker" for the AI-tax-agents. 😂 

It sounds dystopian - things are getting very weird - but this is what we should expect AI to need from us when we can't give it legal personhood.

Until the law recognises machine agency, we can strategically reposition ourselves.

We no longer “do the work.”
Instead - we “own the consequences.”

Which, I truly reckon, is how professionals should operate. We are responsibility for hire. 

If an AI agent wants to give tax advice and get paid for it, it needs a qualified human willing to confirm it and sign it!

#Bitcoin can't be regulated and doesn't need to be. That's the starting point. Everything adjacent to Bitcoin needs regulation to the extent that it departs from Bitcoin's security model, and anything with a foundation to varying degrees. 

That's why I like Stacks, which inherits Bitcoin's security model (PoX on PoW) - it makes it easier to identify the regulatory points. Its native gas token STX is not intended to be used as currency. Bitcoin is, but not natively - through sBTC with a 1:1 non-custodial peg to the user's bitcoin.

 Other Bitcoin L2s offer different ways to use btc productively too - explore beyond Lighting for your use case and risk appetite.

So when stablecoins switch from USD rails to Bitcoin-pegged rails - how a Bitcoin Standard emerges - we should hope to see tokens like sBTC replacing USDC etc, but working in much the same way, with a clear regulatory framework around them.... and no way for any government to expand the underlying money supply. 

This is the visionary approach I'm taking for accounting & finance professional education in Bitcoin.

Also considering decentralised governance/regulatory models for human delegation of authority to AIs that transact with Bitcoin derivatives and all other crypto that should be held to Bitcoin's unit-of-account.

Yes, I cancelled many webinars and podcast interviews in 2025.
And no - it wasnât because I had nothing to say. It was because I wasnât ready to say itâ¦ and my head was exploding. 

I spent 2025 restructuring my physical life while digging myself deeper into the digital rabbit hole - following Bitcoin, AI, network states, and their growing relevance to the trajectory of the accounting profession - while patching together a thesis that now feels too impactful to delay.

I still donât know exactly how to present it. But Iâm ready to make one thing clear:

ð Bitcoin isnât something finance and accounting professionals can keep disregarding or casually lumping in with "crypto" and blockchain. Itâs first principles stuff we need to understand properly, professionally, and in our own language.

So in 2026, Iâm not waiting for the âperfect formatâ or a finished product. Iâm sticking to my audience - public accountants - and sharing everything Iâve learned (and am still learning), inviting their input as I develop this work further.

To start, Iâm hosting a four-part discussion series:
 ðï¸ Bitcoin Insights for Public Accountants

We're not going to talk about accounting software or tax. 
Weâre going to talk about the future relevance of our profession - its existential purpose - and why Bitcoin affirms our core competencies, especially in what we provide to society:

ð§ accountability
ð¦ advocacy
ð¨ trust
ð© verification

To be clear - Iâm not trying to sell Bitcoin. Iâm speaking to the profession that has the most reason to #StudyBitcoin and the most to lose by ignoring it.

If youâre an accountant, this is your territory.

ð§ And to kick off the year, Iâll also be joining Jordan Guess, CPA tomorrow on his Bitcoin for Financial Services show (subscribe in comments!) which promises to be a riveting conversation.

Please leave a comment for reach, when you register. ð

I am Electra Frost. I believe in open, permissionless systems, and since September 2024 I have been living and working among the techno-optimists at Network School in Malaysia. I also live in Fremantle in Western Australia, where I'll soon pilot a similar path: Freo Neuhaus. Balaji Srinivasan founded Network School in Malaysia's Johor-Singapore SEZ as an experiment in seeding a startup society that can bootstrap other startup societies which may eventually become connected as Network States. Freo Neuhaus is my attempt to seed a node of that network in Fremantle, WA. Fremantle has the cultural conditions for this: a port city with a history of radicalism, creative industries, and openness to experiment. It also has the infrastructure: coworking spaces, a university, a startup community, and proximity to Perth's growing tech sector. The idea is to bring together people who are building internet-first businesses, studying frontier technology, or designing new governance models, and create the conditions for them to live and work together in a way that accelerates all of their projects. Not a conference. Not a hackathon. A sustained community with shared infrastructure, shared learning, and shared accountability to a set of values. The accounting and professional services component is important. Network societies need people who understand how value flows, how obligations are structured, and how compliance works across jurisdictions. A network society without professional services infrastructure is a society that cannot account for itself.
title: Freo Neuhaus: the Human Spark Comes to Port

text:
Australian accountants and tax advisers: would you know if your clients have used Bitcoin or other crypto to secure loans, and whether a CGT event has already happened? Our crypto-rich but cash-poor clients need to know that we know it's a thing!

In this CPD webinar with Advisers Digest Pty Ltd, Iâll walk through the ATOâs current position on crypto-backed lending, referring to their latest reasoning on these specific arrangements. Weâll unpack how legal and beneficial ownership are regarded when crypto moves into different lending environments, what that means for CGT, and what clients should look out for.

Weâll cover:
ð¤ How to recognise when beneficial ownership passes to a lender or protocol, versus when it remains with your client
ð¤ Custodial lending vs non-custodial DeFi lending/borrowing, and why the CGT outcome can diverge
ð¤ When interest and fees on crypto-backed loans are deductible, including loans used to pay expenses and tax debts

The goals are to:
â¨ confidently advise clients of the ATO's position on crypto-backed lending
â¨ help you avoid unexpected CGT audits
â¨ consider crypto-backed lending in cashflow and tax planning
â¨ give you context to understand emerging financial technologies and how they interact with our existing tax law concepts.

Booking link: https://advisersdigest.com.au/when-crypto-lending-triggers-cgt-what-accountants-must-know-november-2025

Please share - this will be a very interesting analysis, and if enough people book in I'll do these crypto tech-tax deep dives more often!

I’ve joined the Western Australian AI Hub as a founding member. 🤖 ✨ 
This new community-led association strives to build WA's AI capability for public benefit, with professional and industry collaboration. 

When I read about the Hub and decided to join it was because I agreed - yes, we really do need to shape our own tools, standards, and knowledge systems rather than importing vendor-led AI solutions that don’t contemplate local needs or long-term consequences.

This is where I put all my energy: shared infrastructure and public interest, with a longgggg-term view. We build our best technology practices through relationships and solving our real problems, with AI integrated into these systems, not brought in as a finished product.

At Network School (NS) in Malaysia, I’m designing new industry pathways for accountants and compliance practitioners working in borderless contexts, using blockchain and AI-based tooling. The WA AI Hub gives me a forum to bring that experience back into WA, and exchange ideas to help me develop more academically informed models.

I see the relationships I form through the WA AI Hub also feeding into an NS-linked local initiative - Freo Neuhaus - launching in the new year as a pop-up neighbourhood in Fremantle, the foundation for a networked creative technologists' hub. An evolution of our Digital Playhouse Foundation vision in Qld, reviving the lineage of the early industrial revolution School of Arts and Bauhaus movements to develop arts-driven entrepreneurship, applied AI, and decentralised public goods infrastructure.

Lots to come! 
Check out Western Australian AI Hub and consider joining - the more sectors, the merrier. They're having an in-person meetup on 12 November.

Thanks Dr Eunice and Josh Tedjasaputra, MSc - it was great to meet you and the members at your AGM last week.

Chinaâs new qualified influencer rule raises a timely question for People of the Free Internet: who decides who we can trust to speak on complex financial, legal, and civic matters?

As of this month, influencers in China cannot discuss regulated fields without verified qualifications. Platforms must check credentials, label AI-generated content, and block disguised advertising. 

Officials say theyâre protecting consumers. Critics see it as speech control.

ð¤· Both arguments matter. 

Misinformation in finance, law, or medicine can cause real harm. But when a state defines âexpertiseâ through bureaucratic authority, objectivity can make way for political convenience.

Professions were designed to be self-regulating for exactly this reason, so that standards, knowledge, ethics, and accountability are maintained by practitioners, not administrators.

Startup societies like 
@ns
 Network School face tension from the opposite direction. They prioritise openness and resist censorship, but without internal mechanisms or precedents for quality control, even well-intentioned contributors can mislead others. Iâve seen unqualified individuals host tax and financial strategy workshops and distribute inaccurate materials that could harm those who act on them.

In a censorship-resistant environment thereâs no clear corrective process, yet harm still occurs. Also, the reputations of qualified people within the community are at risk if they stay silent.

Some form of regulation is necessary, but in a social experiment it develops gradually. Authority in specialised areas should come from those with proven competence, recognised within the community.

Iâd like to see community-level expertise governance: a peer-to-peer model where professionals assess one anotherâs competence, granting authority based on capability rather than administrative or funded position.

Such systems could validate emerging areas of practice, like crypto accounting, fintech regulation, digital compliance, through the groups actively developing those domains. 

To function as genuine public goods, they'd be transparent and free from financial or political influence.

ð®ââï¸Political systems enforce compliance. 
ð§âðProfessional systems pursue truth.

ðFor startup societies to evolve into credible, diplomatically recognised Network States will depend on maintaining that distinction and ensuring that self-regulation genuinely safeguards their communities.

ð§Iâm firmly aligned with Network State worldview of decentralised governance and the idea that the internetâs self-regulatory capacity should outcompete state control. 

This post isnât a critique of China either; itâs a case study in why professional self-governance is essential for emerging societies of the internet age.

Thoughts?

China’s new qualified influencer rule raises a timely question for People of the Free Internet: who decides who we can trust to speak on complex financial, legal, and civic matters?

As of this month, influencers in China cannot discuss regulated fields without verified qualifications. Platforms must check credentials, label AI-generated content, and block disguised advertising. 

Officials say they’re protecting consumers. Critics see it as speech control.

Both arguments matter. 

Misinformation in finance, law, or medicine can cause real harm. But when a state defines “expertise” through bureaucratic authority, objectivity can make way for political convenience.

Professions were designed to be self-regulating for exactly this reason,so that standards, knowledge, ethics, and accountability are maintained by practitioners, not administrators.

Startup societies like Network School face tension from the opposite direction. They prioritise openness and resist censorship, but without internal mechanisms or precedents for quality control, even well-intentioned contributors can mislead others. I’ve seen unqualified individuals host tax and financial strategy workshops and distribute inaccurate materials that could harm those who act on them.

In a censorship-resistant environment there’s no clear corrective process, yet harm still occurs. The reputations of qualified people within the community are at risk if they stay silent.

Some form of regulation is necessary, but in a social experiment it develops gradually. Authority in specialised areas should come from those with proven competence, recognised within the community.

I’d like to see community-level expertise governance: a peer-to-peer model where professionals assess one another’s competence, granting authority based on capability rather than administrative or funded position.

Such systems could validate emerging areas of practice, like crypto accounting, fintech regulation, digital compliance, through the groups actively developing those domains. 

To function as genuine public goods, they'd be transparent and free from financial or political influence.

Political systems enforce compliance. 
Professional systems pursue truth.
For startup societies to evolve into credible, diplomatically recognised Network States will depend on maintaining that distinction and ensuring that self-regulation genuinely safeguards their communities.

I’m firmly aligned with the Network State worldview of decentralised governance and the idea that the internet’s self-regulatory capacity should outcompete state control.

 This post isn’t a critique of China either; it’s a case study in why professional self-governance is essential for emerging internet societies.

Thoughts?

What’s the difference between a tax accountant and a tax adviser who’s an accountant?

My friend Simon, a lawyer, didn’t understand why I corrected him when he introduced me to people as a tax accountant. Here’s how we cleared it up.

A tax accountant is a qualified accountant, typically IPA, CPA, or CA, registered with the Tax Practitioners Board to prepare and lodge tax documents. They apply the law to what’s already happened and keep clients compliant. It’s exacting work that keeps the tax system running and deserves far more respect than it gets.

A tax adviser who’s an accountant works further upstream - interpreting legislation, structuring transactions, and planning to optimise outcomes while preventing problems before they start. Postgrad tax law and Tax Institute membership are standard.

From an entrepreneurial angle, I’m a tax adviser and accounting technologist. I design service models that close market gaps and turn them into repeatable systems.

An accounting technologist goes beyond using software - working with software vendors and developers (and now vibe coding!) to design new systems, workflows, and service models that change how accounting services are defined and delivered.

That's how I sold my digital tax accounting practice five years ago and why I’m now designing KIGUMI with this lawyer!

Exciting big new business model loading in Port Fremantle.

So while all your Xeros, Asanas, cloud software and blockchain L2's are down - because they depend on AWS (Amazon Web Services) - it's a great time to #StudyBitcoin and decentralised computing infrastructure #DePIN

Yesterday I presented on one of the most confusing areas for digital nomads in Malaysia: individual tax obligations.

We covered how Malaysian tax residency works, what income is assessable, what’s exempt, how to protect yourself from unlicensed tax advice, and prevent unexpected double tax exposure - especially when you're here long-term on a digital nomad visa.

I focused on personal tax compliance - not the sexy structuring stuff of tax - because that's where most get caught out. Many founders are told to just "set up a company in a tax-friendly zone"… but no one explains the personal tax consequences of being physically here, regardless of where payments happen.

This is where I can really help by:
🧚‍♀️Turning complex tax rules into clear, logic trees of defensible position.
 🧚‍♀️ Helping people feel informed and prepared - not scared.
🧚‍♀️ Designing systems that keep individuals safe while they scale globally
🧚‍♀️ Using my trusted adviser network effects - within Malaysia and globally.

Thanks to everyone who attended, asked great questions, and shared what they’re facing. The Network School team's trust and positive-sum attitude make this work worth doing.

More to come.... I’m working on something big behind the scenes.
😉

Tax and visa concerns are closely tied.
If you’re unsure where you stand with your own residency or international income, feel free to comment or DM. Especially if you're interested in coming to Network School to build your project - and want to get it right.

Sudden switch to Founder Mode! 

Since Wednesday's call for help on LinkedIn, Iâve been inundated with support from my global network of tax professionals and builders. Deep thanks to everyone who responded. ð¤

 I'm now designing a scalable model to manage defensible, global tax compliance for the nomadic crypto netizens building at Network School.

This work started by personally vetting known service providers and engaging with the best dark talent here, who are as invested in the Network State vision as I am.

The "free AI tax planning tool" tool being circulated is exactly what I feared: a form that funnels user data into a generic language model, spits out a templated âideal tax setup,â then pushes it to an email.

Basic as.... No logic engine, professional oversight, or audit trail.... resulting in incomplete, legally wrong advice with a marketing lead.

In Australia, we call that Lipstick on a Pig. ð·ð

We accountants - the real ones - have been designing logic, systemising data, and adapting workflows to internet applications for decades. We know how to responsibly use AI. So I'm provoked into action by this.

As one AI tax software developer here agreed with me: the questions donât lead to defendable outcomes. The sources arenât cited. Thereâs no structured knowledge, no logic layer, and no review by qualified professionals. Itâs just generic autocomplete.

This is how people get hurt by the tax man. Hurt bad. 

We can build a proper system: structured by professionals, logic-driven, auditable, and up to legal standards. Every step from user input to advice output, and its deployment, must be verifiable.

This isn't exactly what I came here to build (decentralised professional association) but it compliments it perfectly, and will help startup societies now.

Letâs go! .. for real.

Feeling professionally unsettled. Need help! 😕
A Network School peer asked me to confirm that holding a Malaysia digital-nomad visa stops their tax residency in their home country. The fact that people might believe that worries me.

🤖 Then I saw why - an AI “tax tool” is being circulated that sends users a free “tax plan” for nomad tax freedom, with no qualifications and no professional validation. I tested it myself and the advice was seriously wrong. For someone less experienced, it could easily build false confidence and lead them to spend thousands on a “solution” that doesn’t actually solve anything.

🌏 I’ve spent >20 years helping founders and globally mobile people navigate tax residency, foreign income, exit taxes, and double-tax agreements. I understand why people fall for the good marketing of simple answers.

🥹 I’ve been the one cleaning up the mess afterwards… and I don’t have the time or emotional energy to do that anymore.

🫥 Cross-border tax is too complex for individuals, and most just want to get on with their lives. It shouldn’t be that way. But pretending it can be avoided is how people end up in serious trouble.

⚖️ Roger Ver, a.k.a. “Bitcoin Jesus,” learned that the hard way. After shifting Bitcoin through offshore companies to avoid exit tax and disregarding his tax adviser, he renounced U.S. citizenship The IRS eventually traced it. He now faces prosecution carrying potential decades in prison for tax evasion and fraud. It’s harsh, but he’s being made an example of - so don’t be like Roger.

🧭 I’m hosting a Nomad Tax Reality Check session at Network School next week to unpack these myths and get real about what Malaysia’s source-based tax system means for people working across borders. 

🚨 I need help now from my professional peers - in Malaysia and around the world - to make sure this community of founders, builders, and global remote workers can access qualified, trusted advice. 

🌏 We have MANY nationalities here!

We can’t stop unqualified promoters from appearing, but we can out-organise them by building a visible, verifiable network of professionals who understand this terrain. And yes, I’m sure the clever people here can build better AI tools that we can actually verify and rely on.

❤️ This is too important to ignore. The freedom so many came here to build depends on getting this right.

I’m an Australian international tax adviser - not yet across every detail of Malaysia’s rules - doing my best, fast. Please let me know before my event what insights and resources you can offer.

Thank you 🙏

Congratulations to Ernie Painter on 40 years with the Institute of Public Accountants! At the IPA WA Fellows Lunch, I enjoyed chatting with Ernie about AI, and his brilliant career spanning banking, superannuation, accounting, and unions.

Our conversation turned to decentralisation and Bitcoin, as I'm often asked to explain it. Ernie raised concern for the future income of his author clients as Big Tech AI exploits their work without regard for copyright. As an arts accountant for 25 years, I share that concern. I began studying blockchain at RMIT in 2018 after seeing how creative technologists could use it to design models that return value from big-tech silos to creators.

I explained how authors and artists could attach their works to programmable digital assets on blockchains under their direct control, enabling AI agents to license them to generative platforms and stream payments directly to their wallets. 

Crypto is internet-native, programmable money - enabling our AIs to transact with other AIs on our behalf. AI canât use bank accounts. It needs crypto.

Decentralised network models like this remove reliance on collection societies or agents - but they can only succeed with regulatory and tax reform that recognises crypto assets as distinct from traditional property and excludes crypto and DeFi transactions from the CGT regime.

Ernie lit up at the idea of technology serving creators. This is why Iâm at Network School - and working to position public accountants as leaders in shaping these transitions and maximising social impact with Bitcoin and blockchain.

Ernie asked me for a book recommendation. I immediately suggested Joanna Pennâs Artificial Intelligence, Blockchain, and Virtual Worlds: The Impact of Converging Technologies on Authors and the Publishing Industry. It clearly explains how authors can harness these tools rather than be displaced by them.

I like to think that Ernie will keep following where our profession is heading for many years to come.
:)

If you're an accountant still brushing off Bitcoin as irrelevant or speculative, you're missing the most important monetary shift of our time - one that directly impacts the wealth, equity, and financial resilience of the clients we serve.

Accounting, at its core, is about truth in value, ethics, integrity of records, and the protection of purchasing power over time. 

Yet the foundation of our current system - fiat currency masquerading as money - is infinite in supply and debased annually by policy decisions far beyond our control.

This hidden taxation through inflation disproportionately harms wage earners, freelancers, and SMEs - while inflating the asset prices held by the already wealthy.

It is a professional accountantâs duty to understand this imbalance, not excuse it.

Bitcoin matters because it reintroduces objective scarcity â the first since the end of the gold standard. It is not a speculative asset. It is a programmable accounting primitive on an immutable, time-stamped ledger.

Fixed in supply (21 million), yet endlessly divisible, auditable in real-time, resistant to manipulation, and borderless by design â Bitcoin offers a new foundation for trust-minimised finance, long-term value preservation, and even public credentialing infrastructure for our profession.

The Supply Pyramid says it all:

Fiat: Infinite
Gold: Scarce
Bitcoin: Fixed

Which would you trust to store value over decades?
Which supports sustainable, intergenerational wealth planning? 

Come on, now... how many negatively geared investment properties should we be enriching banks through instead of simply stacking sats??

Bitcoin-native financial instruments, crypto accounting systems, micropayment rails, decentralised professional networks, AI authentication, and timestamped attestations are already emerging.

âï¸ The accountants who study this now will be the ones designing the future of our role. 
âï¸ The rest will keep reporting inflation-distorted figures on a ledger that no longer tells the truth. âï¸Which will serve the public interest - and be recognised for it?

This isn't about ideology. It's about relevance.

Bitcoin is no longer fringe. It's the foundation layer of the next monetary era.

We should be the ones documenting it, and framing its relevance to our profession. Now.

#BitcoinIsAccounting
#AccountantsAreBitcoin
#StudyBitcoin
#TalkAboutBitcoin

 'Outlook on the Future of The Bitcoin Ecosystem' with Jennifer, Odinsmart CMO, from China - bridging web2 users to web3 with #BTCFi - and Piyish, Core Contributor of BIMA BTC lending. 

They shared very spirited views and enthusiasm about how Bitcoin will be used in the next 5-10 years with the improving infrastructure being built for people to use their Bitcoin commercially, profitably and securely in new ways - including:

- Bitcoin-Native Stablecoins 
- secure subnets for making companies' BTC treasuries productive,
- AI-agent assisted identity verification for trust (think decentralised KYC),
- hands-on user education (learn by doing),
- community standard setting (self-regulated industry), 
- and governments "getting on board". 

I had challenged them to convince me that Bitcoin positive accountants (like me) could confidently steer their clients (holding BTC reserves) to Bitcoin DeFi and was impressed to delve deeper! 

Thanks Ar. Kim Zhao Wei and Omnity Network for your excellent organising. It was the most memorable experience for my first day in Hong Kong.

Letâs see what #BTCFi is really made of.ð
Iâm heading to Runes Asia 2025 (Hong Kong) next week to moderate a panel of Bitcoin DeFi developers on the future of the Bitcoin ecosystem - it's exciting to learn about BTC financial utility on Runes and I have lots of questions.

As a professional advisor, I'm exploring how traditional stores-of-value like stocks, real estate, collectibles etc are securely converted into Bitcoin-native digital forms. This is about making BTC productive in decentralised apps and agentic AI-business models, and to earn yield, unlock liquidity, and borrow against (for stablecoins, mortgages and such) - I want to know:

Weâre building all this "for the people".
So how does this infrastructure serve a broader market?
What can we actually do with self-custodied Bitcoin that works for us economically compared to traditional or custodied investment?

And how are we going to onboard everyone? ð

Iâm living, researching, and building a community of users and educators at Network School (Western Australia and Malaysia). I take these questions seriously - because Iâm living them!

ð SOHO House, Hong Kong
ð August 27, 2025
ð Free registration: https://lu.ma/moeuigla

Bitcoin’s strength is monetary integrity, not day-to-day payments. Forcing it into retail transactions ignores what the market actually wants: dollar stability for spending, Bitcoin for saving.

Stablecoins are already handling the transactional layer - cheap, fast, familiar - and are attached to the current global reserve asset and currencies. They’re not competition; they’re infrastructure. They help onboard users without asking them to take on short term volatility up front.

When Bitcoin becomes the base layer, global reserve asset, stablecoins will have done the bridging. Trying to make BTC the point-of-sale token right now is premature and distracts from the bigger goal: building a sound financial foundation on Bitcoin the network.

BTC-for-payments isn't essential - what matters is that everything settles back to Bitcoin's fixed supply. 

Let's build THAT. 
Who is building that already? 🙂

I'm pleased to have been quoted making this important distinction when advising SMSF trustees: “Bitcoin and crypto are not interchangeable terms from an investor perspective. Bitcoin is the only asset that has emerged from the crypto ecosystem with a credible claim to being a long-term store of value.” 

Bitcoin’s proof-of-work and UTXO-based security model give it enduring integrity - the basis for the trust and long-term value that conservative investors seek. That’s why SMSF trustees should treat Bitcoin differently from everything else in “crypto” when preparing their investment strategies, alongside other considerations like custody and trustee experience.

Australian Public Accountants, CPAs, CAs: is it time we step back from "facilitating legal or financial arrangements" that fall into AML/CTF territory? With Tranche 2 reforms coming, I’m re-evaluating services like:
- Entity setup 
- Acting as ASIC agent, registered office, handling correspondence
- Structuring advice or involvement in offshore arrangements
- Assisting with bank or crypto accounts
- Using outsourced formation agents
- Bookkeeping/VCFO work involving software with payment initiation for approval

I’ve appreciated the service providers that have supported our roles in providing ASIC agent functions,  but I’ve never been fully comfortable. This year, I plan to go hands-off and collaborate with a commercial lawyer to look after my clients.

But if we all step back, will we just see more clients trying to set things up themselves without legal advice, which may be cost prohibitive - and us only catching the fallout at tax time?

Curious how others are thinking this through.

Why we are building Network State at Network School ns.com - my key takeaways from Balaji Srinivasan's orientation talk for our 100 new techo-optimist June recruits. ð¤©

Think of a country that starts with internet communities - bringing the cloud to land. Internet-First is like a new political term for a new version of Nationhood. 

It was easier to create Bitcoin than to reform the Fed. And itâs easier to build Network States than to reform broken Nation States. The internet is now the only counterweight to China-scale power. Not adversarial - small nations with a flag but no money are warming to the crypto economy and will welcome NS Nodes.

Think of Network State as a globally distributed country - like a digital archipelago with economic mass.

We arenât building a utopia. Boring tech people are building boring, resilient, economically viable jurisdictions - enterprise-grade SAAS meets jurisdiction-as-a-service. Think startups, DAOs, professionals, developers, and service operators improving on democracy - structuring legitimacy on-chain, with governance primitives and auditable logic.

China can build the most megacities, factories, and warships - but it canât build Bitcoin and freedom tech. It canât build the freewheeling, opt-in logic of the global internet. What China is to scale in the physical world, the internet is to scale in the digital one. The new balance isnât the US vs China. Itâs China vs the Global Internet. 

The US no longer wants to be the centre of the global economy. Business is moving to Asia. But the internet moves faster - itâs already rebalancing global capital and control. Itâs where we build.

Internet First is now a political identity. Itâs more than communications - itâs money (Bitcoin), contract law (blockchain), democratic participation (DAOs), and equal citizenship (peer-to-peer). No visas. No borders. No second-class citizens.

The cloud forms the land. We are building Internet Places: nodes, campuses, jurisdictions, zones. A new internationalism - coded, composable, and aligned to open values.

Network School is where we experiment, co-design and train the builders of these nodes. The builders include accountants, lawyers and engineers of a borderless system. The only thing with the scale to balance Chinaâ¦ is us.

The scale of what we can do on the Internet in 20 years is phenomenal. Just look at the last 20 years and add Bitcoin!

Here in Malaysia/Singapore we are building a startup society to build and bootstrap other startup societies... as nodes, that could all be very different to each other! (cultures, lifestyle etc)

People keep talking about using Bitcoin for payments. This idea is holding us back. 

Jack Dorsey recently said: “I think it has to be payments for [Bitcoin] to be relevant on the everyday.”

I fundamentally disagree.

The next wave of adoption isn’t about buying coffee. It’s about unlocking real utility. Bitcoin is a generational asset. Most holders don’t want to spend it; they want to preserve it. 

So the real question becomes: how do you make Bitcoin useful without spending or selling it?

That’s where Bitcoin DeFi comes in. Today, you can bridge your Bitcoin to Layer 2s and borrow stablecoins against it. No selling. No leaving the Bitcoin ecosystem. Yet, according to Binance, only 0.8% of Bitcoin is being used in DeFi. That’s less than 1% of nearly $1 trillion in onchain capital sitting idle.

As Bitcoin’s block rewards shrink every halving, miner incentives will need new sources of fuel. Scaling to Bitcoin L2s like Stacks is how we get there without compromising the base layer.

If we want Bitcoin to succeed long term, we need to help people do more onchain—lend, borrow, earn, build, not just spend.

The idea of Bitcoin as “digital gold”? It’s already here. Countries are holding it in their strategic reserves. That doesn’t scream “let's buy a latte” to me. It screams, "let's make Bitcoin productive".

I do agree with Jack on one thing: Bitcoin must be relevant in daily life. But relevance doesn’t only come from spending. It comes from functionality. The future of Bitcoin isn’t just hodling or spending it. It’s building real utility and earning on it. Builders who enable real utility for Bitcoiners without compromising its core will shape the next decade.

Let Bitcoin be the store of value it was born to be. Then let the Bitcoins flow 🟧

Full piece on CoinDesk https://t.co/5jJmVUbSE0

My notes from Alex’s talk in our Network School Library today:

Problem:
Frontier AI is being captured by a few big labs.
Training costs are so high, with massive staffing and energy costs, that only the most resourced corporations can participate.
Competitors need $50m just to get started. If you don’t win first, you lose everything. No second play. Shut down. Game over.

“Open source” in AI is a façade. You can’t fork Llama.
Public lock-out on model development. You can’t participate unless you’re inside a core research team.
Meta competes on openness but not capability.
The open vs closed gap is widening fast. 

Our culture is already being shaped by a handful of model owners.
People will learn, decide, and build under models they cannot interrogate.
The effect will be like TikTok ×100 on public thinking.
Deplatforming of businesses and loss of creators' authenticity are inevitable at this scale.
Attempts at regulatory capture have already been made — and will be made again.
Dollar auction dynamics guarantee centralisation will accelerate unless stopped.

Solution:
Decentralised Protocol Learning.
* Distributed training
* Verifiable contribution
* Fractional ownership
* Unextractable weights
* Crypto incentives to sustain decentralised AI

Building the biggest global model economy that is open, permissionless, and sustainable.

Our Biggest Fight warns us all in plain language. Alex’s technical talk today clarified for me how we'll use crypto and decentralised approaches to recapture our future at the AI model layer - and why the threat is bigger and coming faster at us than most realise.

But we have to get this right, with the right incentive model, because real open AI will be unstoppable and there'll be no going back to fix it.

Establishing Bitcoin as a global unit of account is inevitable while central bank monetary policy continues to devalue fiat and we move toward a Bitcoin Standard. With a fixed supply, Bitcoin becomes a neutral, incorruptible measuring stick for all economic output.
📏
At full monetisation, 1 BTC ≈ US$5.5 million (based on 2025 global GDP of ~US$115.5T), making 1 sat ≈ $0.05 - too large for many real-world transactions. Lightning enables millisats (1/1,000 of a sat), but only off-chain. The base layer cannot process sub-sat units. 
⛓️
To support finer denominations with Bitcoin finality - for all means of exchange, not just in BTC or sats - we need anchored, programmable layers like Stacks. These enable BTC-pegged tokens to function as currency, support lending and stable instruments, and preserve the integrity of the base asset. 
🌏
If Bitcoin is to serve as a global unit of account, it requires on-chain layers that extend its utility without compromising its monetary integrity. That’s what platforms like Stacks offer.
📚
Everyone will "get it" when the world's accountants get it. That’s why my focus at Network School is on contextualising the Global Accountants Competencies Framework for a Bitcoin economy. 
🚀

Jack Dorsey's encouragement to use BTC for everyday payments is problematic. He should champion Bitcoin's pivotal function as global Unit of Account, and stressing the importance of Bitcoin L2s like Stacks and its sBTC 1:1 trustless pegged asset approach in achieving that.

Bitcoin is the world's no.1 ledger - not a payments platform - it's the most secure settlement layer for ALL digital currencies and financial instruments on ALL chains to be held to account by, valued, measured, and trusted. 

It's not a mere means of exchange - its far more than currency - it's the most significant monetary technology. 

We're not playing shop with sats anymore! 

By holding any store of value in BTC, we are part owners of the future decentralised world bank ...we'll earn network fees from settlements. That's what "be your own bank" really means.

With that in mind, expecting people to spend sats now is quite frankly deluded. 

Without Bitcoin as our Unit Of Account for all currencies, to eventually flip the USD, every MoE adoption attempt will fail, putting a Bitcoin Standard further out of reach. That's because businesses cannot use BTC as standard for pricing, contracts and financial reporting (e.g. El Salvador).

Why do Bitcoin L2's matter?

“In a few decades when the reward gets too small, the transaction fee will become the main compensation for nodes (miners). I'm sure that in 20 years there will either be very large transaction volume or no volume.”

- Satoshi Nakamoto. bitcointalk. org

Lightning is not the only L2. In the next 5-10 years people will want to make their BTC programmable and collatorise it, not spend it. For "very large transaction volume" look beyond Lightning - the larger Bitcoin L2 ecosystem since Ordinals and AI woke up the Bitcoin builders ... the network fees and latent liquidity of Bitcoin Economy signals!

GovFi: A Decentralized Model for Government Efficiency - crowdsourcing government functions to citizens and rewarding verified contributions, reducing bureaucratic waste, fraud, and inefficiency. Instead of funding bloated public services through excessive taxation and borrowing, this system incentivizes real outcomes through decentralized, transparent processes.

The machine is Pacioli.ai, a decentralized physical infrastructure network 
hashtag
#DePIN built by Auditchain Labs AG. By automating financial and regulatory oversight using AI and blockchain, costly administrative layers are replaced with a distributed validation system. Node operators verify machine-readable disclosures, creating integrity while slashing compliance costs.

When working on CREDU I envisioned a Decentralized Professional Association (DPA) of Accountants forming, reinventing regulations, and claiming a social mandate to take over the functions of tax agencies like the IRS, ATO, and HMRC. 

They told us we'd be the obsolete ones. Wrong. 😉

Instead of taxpayers funding inefficient government agencies, tax and economic governance would be handled peer-to-peer, with verified, on-chain accountability.

Taxation becomes a fraction of the cost, compliance is real-time and accurate, and public financial oversight is restored to qualified people - away from government employees and corporate contractors.

🟧 Big Citizen, Small Government. 

The technology is here. Now, will governments make way for it? They will eventually if we get the public onside and behave competitively.

Last year, I joined Balaji Srinivasan's Network School V1 (Sept–Dec 2024) in Singapore/Malaysia, a social experiment to initiate the emergence of network nations - as cloud communities materialising in the physical world to form startup societies.

After years of isolation living in remote Australia and collaborating online, I thrived in this communal environment - surrounded by high-energy, mission-driven people who inspired and supported my true purpose! 🟧 😎 

We lived, worked, learned, and built on our ideas together - without the usual distractions of running a household. Meals, gym, cleaning, and workspaces were all provided, allowing us to focus on our businesses and projects. 

🏫 Now, Network School is evolving:

🔹 V1 – Startup Society (2024): The inaugural 128 person cohort of 'dark talent' cohort gathered in Malaysia to test this new model of community - combining education, work, and governance in a shared physical space.

🔹 V2 – Society-as-a-Service (2025): This year-long cohort from March 2025 expands to 256 members, forming a permanent, location-flexible community.

🔹 V3 – Society from Scratch (2025+): A permanent Network School campus near Singapore will take shape, designed to house thousands of technologists and innovators.

🔹 V4 – Scaling the School: Prefabricated construction will make replication easy, allowing similar communities to be built worldwide.

🔹 V5 – Start Your Own Society: Members will take these learnings to launch their own startup societies, backed by a global network.

-----------------

✈️ After working here in Perth, I’ll return to Network School V2/V3 - this time, contributing to their plans for building a permanent campus. What might a futurist accounting faculty look like in this new Harvard...? 🤔 

🌏 The idea of scoping a Perth location for V4/V5 also intrigues me. It’s in the same time zone as NS Singapore/Malaysia, making it an easy extension of the network for me as an Australian.

🏘️ This is more about changing HOW I live than WHERE I live. Eventually, with a global diaspora of like-minded people in connected locations enabling digital ownership, a parallel economy, and new ways to create and earn, I see the traditional household becoming unnecessary.

Bitcoin in the Mist: High Altitude Perspective 🍵 🏔️ The most delightful time I had in Malaysia was in the Cameron Highlands, a mountainous plateau where the air was crisp and cool, tea plantations stretched into the clouds, their mist rolling in like something out of a dream.

It’s a funny place - Tudor-style architecture rising from the rainforest, relics of an old British world that persist in the heart of Malaysia.

Exploring winding roads and hidden paths through the tea plantations, disappearing into the mist… you’d climb without knowing what lay ahead, expecting something worthwhile to eventually reveal itself.

Strawberries grow abundantly in the terraced farmlands of the valleys, thriving in this strange highland microclimate. They're made into the most genuinely strawberry-flavoured ice cream in the world - so fresh, so real, the taste still lingers.

Climbing that misty path, barely able to see a metre ahead, reminds me of Bitcoin adoption - the uncertainty, the need for conviction, and the decision to ascend without guarantees.

Bitcoin is not an endpoint; it is a process of discovery. The path forward is uncertain, but the principles remain clear.

Some hesitate at the base, waiting for perfect clarity - regulations, mainstream adoption, the familiar structure of traditional finance. Others have already started climbing, believing that the path is worth following, even if the summit is not yet visible.

You don’t wait for absolute clarity before moving; you move because the direction is right.

We have two choices:
✅ Remain anchored in legacy systems, in the comfort of what has always been.
✅ Step into something new, uncertain, but full of potential.

The fog will clear for everyone eventually, but by then, the best vantage points will already be taken.

Bitcoin rewards conviction. So does life.

Those who seek safety in old systems will remain anchored to the past. Those who embrace uncertainty will build the future.

The best spots at the summit won’t belong to those who waited for the view to be obvious.

They will belong to those who climbed into the unknown when others stood still. 

Some paths only reveal their value after you start walking. 

🤔 ❓ What makes someone take the first step? Thinking on this... 
✍

ð¨ Australia's Financial Reporting Future â Where Are the Digital Assets? ð¨
Treasuryâs consultation on merging the FRC, AASB, and AUASB into a new statutory body sets out priorities including sustainability reporting, public sector financial reporting, audit and assurance standards, and alignment with IFRS. Yet, digital asset financial reporting is completely absent! ð¥¶ 

Ignoring digital assets at this stage is a serious oversight. Other jurisdictions are already integrating digital asset financial reporting into their regulatory frameworks, so accounting professionals are equipped to address the complexities of Bitcoin, tokenised finance, stablecoins, and blockchain-based financial instruments.

If this new statutory body is to position Australiaâs financial reporting for the future, digital asset reporting must be part of its mandate. The merged entity must have a DIGITAL ECONOMY REPORTING STANDARDS COMMITTEE within its governance structure and ensure the technical committee includes expertise in:

ð On-chain financial reporting
ð Proof-of-reserves methodologies
ð Blockchain based audit tools and financial controls
ð Subledger reconciliation for digital assets
ð FASB ASU 2023-08 and IFRS discussions on fair value accounting
ð A deep understanding of the unique characteristics of Bitcoin and DeFi
... and more

Without this, Australia will lack the necessary financial reporting standards for the digital economy, putting accountants at a disadvantage and creating regulatory uncertainty for businesses operating in this space.

The Board's strategic direction and its technical committees should make digital asset reporting a primary focus area of standard-setting in the new body. 

This is a once is a generation opportunity for Australia to establish a modernised financial reporting framework! 

Treasury is seeking submissions - I encourage accountants to engage and push for digital asset reporting to be included.

Go here: https://treasury.gov.au/consultation/c2025-536708

A Bitcoiner in Malacca: Lessons in Misplaced Trust: When I wandered Malacca’s streets, its history struck a chord. In 1511, the Portuguese arrived in Malacca, presenting themselves as allies offering collaboration and mutual benefit. The Malaccans, operating in good faith, welcomed what appeared to be a promising opportunity. 

However, beneath the surface, the Portuguese were strategising a take over. They exploited Malacca’s openness and weaknesses, and conquered them, marking the beginning of its colonisation and delivering a harsh lesson about misplaced trust.

Bitcoin, founded on principles of openness, decentralisation, and independence, is designed to eliminate the need for trust. Yet, like Malacca’s leaders welcoming the Portuguese to a seat at their table, Bitcoiners today are accepting political figures like Trump, attracted by his rhetoric on freedom and innovation.

As history shows, not everyone acts in alignment with their words - what seems like support may instead lead to harm. The lesson here to scrutinise motives and core values before giving anyone a foothold in our communities. 

Malacca’s story, like the Bitcoin community's, reminds me of the importance of protecting what we’ve built from those who see opportunities to exploit rather than strengthen.

Beautiful city - needs at least 2 days to explore properly so I'll head back this year :)

...to be continued
✍

Like a modern-day Tintin, I’m setting off on new adventures as a digital nomad (or slowmad), to chart my path globally and share my journey along the way. Selling my house and all my possessions, reduced to two suitcases. I’ll own everything digitally and I’ll be happy!

Life on remote coastal acreage in Queensland since leaving Sydney in 2016 was an amazing chapter. Living off-grid with mountain biking to deserted beaches, drinking rainwater, growing food, creating Digital Playhouse Foundation with🟧Gordon Christian, mining Bitcoin with solar, and collaborating with Bitcoin and web3 visionaries around the world while running my online accounting practice has been ideal in many ways.

But it hasn’t been without challenges. Pre-COVID, I balanced my country life with an apartment in Brisbane, but since then, isolation crept in. Even with great local friends and travels to events, I felt the loneliness acutely after returning from El Salvador in 2022. I missed the hardcore social purpose, Bitcoin hackathons, and entrepreneurial spirit I found there, and it made me realise how stuck I’d become in my "remote paradise." I stayed, but I stagnated - feeling trapped by my house, possessions, and personal circumstances.

That all shifted in 2024. After meeting up at WAWEB3 in Perth, my friend Andrew Noble suggested we go to the Network State Conference in Singapore, to learn about what's happening with Balaji Srinivasan's vision of decentralised, borderless societies. A few weeks before the event, I applied to join the Network School cohort - Balaji's 90-day startup-society cohort on an island near Singapore - and was accepted!

While living communally with digital nomads and "slowmads" from around the world, I discovered the parallel societies movement. This is far more significant than digital nomadism - they're physically iterating borderless, global communities that I can contribute to. 

Network School changed my perspective of "home" from nationhood to networkhood. I never consented to be defined by the country I was born in. I'm free to align myself with an internet-based society of shared values, ambitions, economy and vision for the future... if I step into it.

Since returning to Australia at Christmas, I’ve been preparing for this migration. Getting rid of all the "stuff" I no longer need, along with lingering self-imposed limitations.

First stop: A few months in Perth for an exciting accounting project and conferences, while waiting for Network School to re-open in Asia. Some pop-up cities in El Salvador and Brazil, with Network School connections, are also calling.

I need to immerse myself in the borderless, Bitcoin shaped future I believe in, and will be sharing my journey - hoping it inspires others to explore new paths too.

(Photo: The TinTin Shop in Singapore. My itinerant parents called me TinTin because I was always exploring, and could be relied on to find my way out of trouble - and back to the hotel.)

I’m passionate about the future of accounting for the decentralized economy and parallel societies, and eager to collaborate with others building for this vision today. 

I see Cryptoworth as an invaluable tool, especially now for DAOs and Web3 enterprises operating without fiat, bank accounts or traditional ledger systems like Xero or QuickBooks (although that integration is there if needed).

We often discuss crypto subledgers, but I’m more interested in its potential as a primary ledger - offering:

⛓️‍💥 Accurate tracking and reporting of on-chain transactions.
⛓️‍💥 Transparency for members and stakeholders, which is central to governance in decentralized organizations.
⛓️‍💥 Customization options to handle high transaction volumes and unique reporting needs.
⛓️‍💥 Support for 800+ blockchains, enabling comprehensive crypto asset management.
⛓️‍💥 Simplified monthly workflows by automating transaction categorization, reconciliation, and reporting from real-time blockchain data.

📊 For DAOs, which run on community consensus and automated processes, reporting is about giving members clear and accurate financial information.

📋 We're not just ticking boxes for government requirements... accountability to our self-regulated communities comes first!

I appreciated how John and Richard emphasised to my clients this focus on financial transparency for members - addressing inefficiencies and enabling DAOs to allocate resources more effectively - that's key to gaining a competitive edge for their communities.

🚀 It's inspiring to see accounting align with the direction of a decentralized global economy, rather than retrofitting outdated systems.

Bitcoin's pivotal monetary function as a Unit of Account redefines how we measure, compare, and transact economic value, to create a consistent global standard. Most people understand Bitcoin as a Store of Value or Means of Exchange, but its potential as a Unit of Account deserves greater exploration. A Unit of Account is one of the foundational functions of money, providing a standard for pricing goods, services, and contracts. It enables us to compare values across time, place, and context. When Bitcoin becomes the UoA, all economic activity is priced in Bitcoin or satoshis, creating a single, universally accessible reference point for value. Accountants are the custodians of value measurement. We design the systems that record, report, and interpret economic activity. If Bitcoin becomes the global UoA, accountants are the professionals who will operationalise that transition - designing the accounting frameworks, the reporting standards, and the compliance systems that make it work in practice. The Composite Fractionalized Money approach: Rather than a binary switch from fiat to Bitcoin UoA, I propose a composite approach in which Bitcoin serves as the reference unit while operational currencies remain the transactional medium. This mirrors how gold once functioned as a reference standard while national currencies handled day-to-day transactions. Every balance sheet, every income statement, every contract would carry a Bitcoin-denominated reference value alongside its nominal currency value. Network accountants - practitioners operating in the Network State context, across jurisdictions, with Bitcoin-native clients - are the natural pioneers of this framework.
title: Bitcoin Unit-of-Account: Establishing a Global Standard with Network Accountants
