In a multi-chain, multi-currency future, Bitcoin serves as our ideal Unit of Account for consistent value measurement and pioneering a new financial paradigm. ​Most see Bitcoin as a Store of Value (SoV) or Means of Exchange (MoE), but its pivotal function as a Unit of Account (UoA) is less explored. How do we get there? A multi-chain approach with composite fractionalised money may make it work.

A UoA is essential for measuring and comparing economic value, providing a standard for valuing goods, services, and transactions—much like the US dollar in pricing and financial reporting. For global "cloud countries," adopting Bitcoin as UoA represents a logical shift away from reliance on the USD as the global reserve asset.

I'm presenting this session at 
hashtag
#TheNetworkSchool to share research on:

​ 🅱️ Bitcoin’s Function as a Unit of Account: 
The foundational purpose of money; the "Standard of Contract" concept; Bitcoin as a global reserve asset and base layer for settlements; Fixed supply and decentralization make it a unique economic standard.

​ 🌴 Lessons from Próspera: 
The first startup society (in Honduras) to pioneer Bitcoin as a Unit of Account - their bold purpose, novel tax system, challenges with eGovernance and accounting integrations, regulatory hurdles.

​ 💳 The Subscription State: 
Composite Fractionalized Money offers an approach to developing the economic framework for a society-as-a-service model using a Bitcoin Unit of Account, addressing the challenges of globally distributed startup societies.

​ ⛓️ Dynamic Payment System Design: 
A smart contract-powered mechanism for Composite Fractionalized Money, integrating sBTC (trustless BTC peg on Stacks) and stablecoins to mitigate volatility. 

​This approach is aimed at:

​ 1️⃣ Achieving consistent value measurement, flexible payments, and predictable economic outcomes for merchants and participants (e.g., Subscription States and members; e.g. Network Schools globally). 

​ 2️⃣ Facilitating practical implementation of Bitcoin as a Unit of Account, globally.

Commonwealth Bank’s duplicate transaction error exposes the risks of centralised banking, where systemic errors and mismanagement can lead to issues like duplicated payments. A Bitcoin-anchored ledger would prevent duplicates by using decentralised consensus, where each transaction is validated, cryptographically secured, and permanently recorded. 

Backed by proof-of-work, code, and maths, a Bitcoin network layer design would ensure that every unit can only be spent once, as any duplication attempts would be detected and rejected by the networks. 

If our money were pegged to Bitcoin, each transaction would be transparent and require network consensus before confirmation, eliminating accidental duplication and bank fraud, and ensuring the integrity of the entire system.

Now envision our money as a token on a Bitcoin Layer 2 network like sBTC on Stacks, but using a zero-knowledge-first chain for customer privacy. This setup would bring Bitcoin finality to bank settlements, ensuring fast, efficient processing while prioritising both security and privacy. 

Irregularities would be detected and stopped on-chain, blending the reliability of traditional banking with Bitcoin’s superior security model. Understanding how Proof-of-Work (Bitcoin) and Proof-of-Transfer (Stacks) operate reveals how this system could also reduce agency costs tied to trust.

Finance and accounting professionals should be advocating for this shift—using Bitcoin-based Layer 2 solutions would provide a more secure, accountable foundation for financial systems, making duplicated transactions and fraud nearly impossible. Extend this Bitcoin Layer 2 model to nation-state treasuries and reserve banks to completely reshape global finance (a truly great reset). 

Plus, banks would see lower operational costs and higher profits, redistributed to network participants. With Bitcoin, you're not just a customer; you're a network member. And that’s where the real value lies.

As the Australian 31 October deadline approaches for self-lodging tax returns, we are jumping into action for our crypto frens in tax trouble. By appointing a registered tax agent before this date, you'll secure an extension, giving you until 15 May 2025 to lodge your return and pay any tax due. 

Got overdue returns? We can sort those out at ElectraFi too, ensuring your crypto transactions are accurately reported and minimising ATO penalties. 

As a registered tax agent since 2007 who is personally invested in the crypto world in a big way, I know how to get your tax down and will go to bat for you with the ATO. So get in touch if you need help.

The poet, DerGigi, underlined Value-4-Value with "Time, Talent, and Treasure"
He emphasised that while "treasure" is welcome, contributions of "time" and "talent" are just as valuable. When people are voluntarily giving back in a decentralised, talent-driven system, a feedback loop of value exchange is created that isn't solely reliant on financial transactions.

I believe that a reputation reward system built on value-4-value principles can incentivise learners to continuously deliver high-quality content and engage their audience in meaningful ways, creating a virtuous cycle of value exchange. 

I explored these ideas with Accountants On-Chain knowledge sharing in 2021-2022, ideas which formed the basis of the open-source CREDU and BITBYBIT projects I'm working on while here.

How can internet commerce overcome the cognitive barriers associated with micropayments to make them a practical option for consumers?

The article "Micropayments and Mental Transaction Costs" from the Satoshi Nakamoto Institute explores why micropayments haven't caught on yet. 

It argues that the primary barrier to the adoption of micropayments is the mental transaction costs incurred by consumers. These costs arise from the cognitive effort required to manage small transactions, which often outweigh the technological benefits of reduced transaction fees. 

The paper distinguishes between technological transaction costs, which have been minimised by micropayment technologies, and cognitive costs, which remain significant. 

It suggests that efforts to reduce these cognitive costs, such as through automation or improved user interfaces, could make micropayments more viable.

However, the challenge remains in effectively communicating consumer preferences to software, which is crucial for reducing their mental accounting burden. 

Highly technical but very important for Bitcoin Builders to consider. 👷‍♀️

 I know that my CREDU solution, by ditching the subscription cost model for sBTC micropayments, will be vastly more cost effective than other CPD tracking and compliance options for professional accounting bodies and their members. But they'd rather spend more on costly legacy internet commerce subscriptions because it's easier to understand. So I'll need a strategy on this.

🤔

#BitcoinNashville Bitcoin Builders Conference Highlights Pt 2 of 4: All Roads Lead to Bitcoin! See some sessions I flagged for digital accounting thought leaders on how Bitcoin is perceived and used, from its global finance role to practical applications in business. (links in comments).

As more institutions recognise Bitcoin as a legitimate asset and seek to unlock liquidity, the Bitcoin community debates how to balance its original ethos of decentralisation with the development of new functionalities that introduce different trust models.

WHAT IS A BITCOIN LAYER?

This discussion evaluated Bitcoin layers and the trade-offs and trust assumptions involved. Andre Serrano explained sBTC, a method for moving BTC into Bitcoin layers, and how Stacks fits into the Bitcoin L2 landscape. 

Janusz Grzegorz is developing a risk analysis framework, and believes that scaling blockchains with blockchains won't work vs centralised systems that can offer people a right to self-custody and privacy. Andre argues that different decentralised solutions can uphold those rights, that Bitcoin should ossify and to keep experimentation off its base layer.

Bitcoin layers extend BTC functionality with smart contracts and offer cheaper, faster payments. Key questions include custody of funds, sovereignty, verifiability on Bitcoin, and settlement finality. If users don't know the risks, can they recover their BTC from the L2?

Accountant POV: I foresee this becoming relevant to financial disclosures of businesses using Bitcoin L2s.

OWNING IT: RESPONSIBLY BUILDING IN BITCOIN

Bitcoin's open protocol invites innovation while placing the onus on personal responsibility and shared accountability. The concept of "coopetition" was raised - builders collaborate while competing within the Bitcoin space to grow its value. Unlike Web2 which relies on regulators, Bitcoinâs free market demands that builders be accountable, with ethical responsibility replacing regulatory oversight.

Web3 leaders coming to Bitcoin must be of good character and reputation but also recognise their duty to build responsibly and ethically. As free market maximalists, we believe the market, not regulators, should enforce this accountability.

Experimentation is key to achieving the 10,000X growth needed to scale a global Bitcoin financial system, with L2's enabling expansion while Bitcoinâs core principles are safeguarded

BNS, THE ORIGINAL ONCHAIN NAMING SYSTEM

The Bitcoin Naming System (BNS) is a truly open, decentralised naming system rooted in Satoshi's vision, originally on Namecoin and Bitcoin L1. Similar to ENS on Eth, BNS allows for tokenised ID's like electrafrost.btc instead of long wallet addresses. The BNS V2.0 upgrade introduces new features and namespaces for marketplace compatibility. 

As we stacks sats for the Internet of Money, could linking traditional domains like electrafrost.com with decentralised IDs through .Locker be the breakthrough orange pill for 370m domain registrants? ð¤

#BitcoinNashville Bitcoin Builders Conference Highlights Pt 1 of 4: All Roads Lead to Bitcoin! As a digital accountant and early web adopter, I'm researching emerging applications in the Bitcoin ecosystem to stay at the forefront of my field because BITCOIN IS THE INTERNET WITH MONEY. 

Bitcoin is the most sound asset in the digital world. Now a borderless financial system of payments, savings, investments, mortgages and digital commerce is being built around this pristine asset.

What does that mean to an accountant/business adviser?
Bitcoin integrates financial capabilities directly into the internet, offering transformative potential for BTC-holding businesses of all sizes to scale competitively. Stacks blockchain for instance, turns a bitcoin store-of-value into programmable digital money at the Layer 2 level. 

Tim Draper stated that "Stacks will be the next Microsoft", believing in Stacks' potential to boom in the blockchain space, much like Microsoft did with software. Draper sees Stacks, a layer 2 bringing smart contracts and dApps to Bitcoin, as a transformative technology to drive widespread adoption of Bitcoin-based applications. 

Stacks leverages Bitcoin's security through its Proof of Transfer (PoX) consensus mechanism and introduces Clarity smart contracts for predictable and secure dApps. Draper envisions Stacks creating an ecosystem similar to Microsoft's, attracting developers and users, to become a cornerstone in the blockchain industry.

Accountants are seeing more clients holding BTC on their balance sheets, and comparing it to digital gold with potential for capital appreciation. Our next step is to understand how this Bitcoin capital can be productively unlocked in various business models. 

Users are coming to Bitcoin with fresh eyes, asking what can be done with this financial asset. Is it too much to ask to borrow or lend our BTC trustlessly? The base capital is there - there's no need to fabricate it with tokens.

In the last 20 years we've guided bricks and mortar business models to adapt to the internet. Next they'll adapt to Bitcoin. Emerging solutions offer users options based on their specific needs for transaction speed, privacy, scalability, and functionality. We must also learn about the risks and trust assumptions down the decentralisation scale.

Our clients may also hold other crypto assets. While Ethereum competes on technical aspects to attract capital, it cannot match Bitcoin's >$1tn market cap. With Layer 2 solutions like Stacks and Lightning Network unlocking that capital by making Bitcoin programmable and vastly transactable, Bitcoin's potential appears magnitudes greater than that of other blockchains. 

If you or your clients hold BTC on your balance sheet - follow this space with me! The more we understand Bitcoin as a technology with programmable digital commodity features, beyond P2P cash and digital gold, the clearer the future of money (and accounting) becomes.

Next post will be about the presentations!

The world changed on Friday, July 19, 2024. People became very aware of the BSOD, also known as the "Blue Screen of Death." But why? Over 8 million computer systems were crippled due to CrowdStrike, a cybersecurity company that is, or was, the leading choice in the industry for many companies all over the world to be safeguarded against hacks. Since 2011, they’ve become a very important player in the tech world. As software companies often do, they pushed the update that was designed to prevent things related to external cyberattacks. Sounds great, right?

Well, this update triggered a logic error that made the native operating system crash. This operating system crash only impacted companies running on Microsoft Windows, not Mac or Linux. This wrecked banks, airlines, gas stations, point-of-sale systems, and more. A loved one from South Africa couldn’t access their bank account. A friend traveling from Washington, DC, had their flight delayed for hours. The biggest threat to a system is its point of failure. CrowdStrike was a single point of failure within many systems around the world. That’s a problem.

This made me wonder: what would have happened if the system was set up in a decentralized way? Bitcoin is not only digital gold, a medium of exchange, immutable, and divisible, but it is also OSS, also known as open source software, that anyone can build upon. A decentralized system like Bitcoin has a key benefit as an alternative system; the lack of a central point of failure. Bitcoin's decentralized network is not controlled by any single entity, so an issue with one component would not bring down the entire system. This may make Bitcoin more resilient to the kind of widespread outage caused by the CrowdStrike update. Bitcoin layers such as Stacks or even infrastructure layers like Arch Network may be able to scale Bitcoin to higher heights.

The acts of few affect the many. This is true, not only in life but also in tech.

If AWS goes down, so do a large number of websites. If JP Morgan fails, this will ripple to many more banks. If America falls, it will have consequences on a global level, especially when looking at monetary policy. The world is getting crazy, so we have to do what I call “Planning to Z”. What are the most important aspects of your life? I frame things like Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs. As a kid in the 80s, I joined my parents at anti nuclear war protests, terrified by mushroom clouds and atomic bomb aftermath on TV. In 2003, my accounting boss brought his kid and we marched with thousands against the Gulf War. In 2021, we stood up against government overreach during the pandemic and faced police brutality. 

Now in 2024, I'm wondering if we'll protest wars when Australian laws can restrict our right to protest under the guise of national security, and THE ADF HAS KILLER DRONES. 😨 

(I don't normally watch TV but saw this news story in the hotel gym today)

As an accountant, I'm preparing to report on companies' ESG scorecards, reflecting their environmental, social, and governance performance. Since KPMG has already educated us on the positive ESG environmental impacts of Bitcoin mining, it's time to broaden the conversation to include how Bitcoin can also support the social aspects of ESG. 

Unlike fiat currencies, whose value is often backed by governments' ability to print money and, by extension, fund wars, Bitcoin's value is backed by proof-of-work and scarcity. Understanding this fundamental difference changes how we think about ethical financial practices.

"Fiat currency was invented to fund the cause of war." Robert Kennedy Jnr

"The goal is to have endless wars. Not successful ones." Julian Assange.

For the first time ever we can demonetise wars by using Bitcoin. Bitcoin prevents governments from funding their ambitions with inflation. 

By printing money out of thin air, governments diminish the value of our fiat currency, which makes life harder for most. A fiat currency enables money printing and tends to support long and costly wars. Bitcoin makes war less durable... because noone can print more of it.

By incorporating Bitcoin into our lives and businesses, and discussing the benefits of a global reserve asset with a fixed supply and decentralised nature, we can promote a fairer financial system that resists inflationary pressures and can bring greater social stability and trust in those who hold public office.

Displaying Bitcoin on the balance sheet can be a strong statement of support for a more sustainable and ethical financial future.

Who's brave enough to protest now? If we feel insecure about our right to protest, it's time we rethink our approach. Integrating Bitcoin into corporate financial practices is another way to limit the unchecked financial power of governments and support a transparent, peaceful and ethical financial system for all. 

With sustainability reporting (ESG), the accounting industry has an opportunity right now to stand for a future where financial practices align with our values of peace and social justice.

Bitcoin laid the foundation, and The Bitcoin Standard supports it, but for us all to truly showcase our Net Assets, we need to tokenize all forms of income-generating assets on blockchain—be they tangible or intangible, whole or fractionalized - with Bitcoin as Unit of Account.

Accountants, business advisers and financial planners must strive to understand and embrace this shift. Them failing to do so holds everyone back.

The future accounting textbooks for the digital asset revolution will be written by graduates of The Accountant Quits' Crypto Accounting Academy. We explored ACCOUNTING FOR ON-CHAIN TRANSACTIONS in Week 2, with Bassil Eid from detof, using his intuitive crypto subledger tool [B](https://www.linkedin.com/company/alwaysbebreezing/)reezing

"For accountants who manage crypto business clients. Not individuals who need a tax return or crypto tracking."

To compare the differences in Balance Sheet presentation, exercises were:
1. Using Xero and creating Chart of Accounts by Token
2. Using Quickbooks and creating Chart of Accounts by Token
3. Using Xero and creating Chart of Accounts by Wallets
4. Using Quickbooks and creating Chart of Accounts by Wallets

We created our test companies in the accounting software with some additions to the COA for crypto, set up the company's digital wallets in the subledger, pulled in some test transactions from the blockchains, checked balances on the block explorers, connected Xero/QB to Breezing crypto subledger, created some accounts rules and mapping, started categorising on-chain transactions (send, receive, swap and internal), synced them to the accounting software and examined the resulting journals. 
Hmmm!

Discussing the pros and cons of booking journal entries by token view vs wallet view was really interesting to me, because I've tended towards a wallet view with my Chart of Accounts. I guess that's just how I self-taught and it was habit. Likewise, setting up Bank Accounts in Xero for crypto rather than Asset Accounts has been the default approach adopted from previous crypto subledger implementations. 

This lesson was valuable to me because it got me thinking about why, and for which types of business operations, different approaches could be appropriate. I think also depends on the software you're using, and what the clients wants to disclose on their financial reports. I'd like to know what you think too!

I'll be pondering all this as I spend the weekend pulling apart my own company's crypto accounting (ElectraFi is my guinea pig) and getting myself up to speed for Part 2 of this habit-challenging module next week!

If you're interested in joining Cohort 4 in July, apply with my referrer link in the comments below :)

I just renewed my 16+ year membership with The Tax Institute. As we await the release of the Board of Taxation's much-anticipated report on the tax treatment of digital assets and transactions, I'm hopeful that it will pave the way for substantive tax legislation that I can use to advise clients with. 

Since 2021, we've waited for much-needed clarity and to move beyond the current interpretations of existing laws by the ATO (and all their non-binding website guidance that we take screen shots of) which often lead to complexities and inconsistencies, resulting in inequitable tax outcomes for crypto taxpayers.

The ATO's guidance for crypto users has been insufficient, primarily focusing on basic fungible tokens and investment activities, which does not cover the broader and rapidly evolving digital asset economy. The tax treatment of crypto assets should not be left solely to ordinary tax principles. 

The current tax treatment of crypto assets is not clear and understood under Australian tax law, leading to significant uncertainty and hesitation among tax practitioners and the public. 

I believe, as tax professionals, we are social advocates. Many of us are advocating for a specific statutory regime or framework for the emerging crypto and digital asset economy, to provide clarity and ensure appropriate tax outcomes.

I thought about quitting the tax profession last year, out of frustration with the growing tardiness of Australia's tax system and pain of dealing with the ATO. In my accounting practice, because we manage our clients' affairs all year round and, with forecasting, tax planning is a continual element of our monthly reporting. Tax is just another business expense we manage. 

However, there's more work to do here for the crypto and blockchain industry.

Sometimes I think about replacing the Tax Practitioners Board with a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) of tax professionals, and potentially the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) as well. It would be a great project for CTAs in blockchain! 

As such, I figured that it is imperative that I remain a member of the TTI for life.

BITCOIN HALVING TODAY! This is a significant event that ensures deflationary effects on our future currencies built on Bitcoin layers ð° 

Bitcoin has a four-year appreciation cycle, and itâs marked by the Halving.

As Bitcoin approaches its 4th halvingâexpected today, just 30 blocks awayâhere's a TLDR version of a ZeroHedge article.

**Bitcoin Halving Simplified**: Every 210,000 blocks, Bitcoin mining rewards halve, slowing the creation of new coins to prolong the mining systemâs viability. This event, occurring roughly every four years, is designed to create scarcity similar to precious metals like gold.

**Effects on Bitcoinâs Value**: Halving typically triggers a price surge in Bitcoin by reducing supply and increasing scarcity, similar to past events where the minerâs reward drop heightened Bitcoin's perceived value.

**Bitcoin vs. Gold**: After the halving, Bitcoin's inflation rate will fall below goldâs near-2% annual increase, suggesting Bitcoin might be a more stable value reserve than traditional assets.

**Future Value Projections**: The article suggests that continual reductions in Bitcoin issuance could drastically boost its price, especially as daily rewards are projected to fall below one Bitcoin by 2060.

**Cultural and Economic Implications**: More than a technical adjustment, the halving signifies a shift towards a decentralized monetary system, potentially redistributing economic power from traditional currencies to Bitcoin.

I'm currently reviewing crypto accounting subledger / back office software for crypto exchanges. As business accountants since 2013 we've typically dealt with clients' crypto payments and reserves, since 2021 with collectable and utility NFTs, web3 start-ups and DAOs, reconciling on-chain and off-chain data using tools that connect with their accounting system. 

Now looking at custody of customer assets and associated liability, we're aware of more complex accounting principles and regulatory risks at play. So it's great to learn how crypto ERP software does FBO accounting ("For Benefit Of" accounts).

Most assume that crypto tax software can do the job. For a small operator with occasional crypto payments, it can, as long as you prepare manual journals in Excel and accounting software. Your double-entry accounting time really blows out once DeFi, NFTs, smart contracts enter the business operations... with multiple wallets to keep track of, and different accounting methods and cost basis. 

That's when a crypto subledger tool becomes essential to automate all this at scale - with consistency and features for internal controls, for mapping all the transactions to the Chart of Accounts in Xero or QB, and running reports. With the right tax reporting functions, no need to keep two sets of accounts.

Founders don't always start off with great wallet hygiene. Tracking revenue separately to treasury activities is a challenge, and multiple data sources require investigation. So setting up an accounting system for a client also involves working with them to update internal finance policies and processes.

Once all the integrations and configurations are complete, import the data, check it and reconcile (sorry, the software doesn't do it all for us!).... and THEN we can streamline a repeatable process for producing an accurate P&L, Balance Sheet, Cash Flow, Budget and Forecast reporting pack EVERY MONTH... with relative ease.

This demonstrates to investors and regulators that you have control over the finances. Every month - check data, reconcile, manual journals, generate reports, analyse and present. That's what it's all about... unless you're FTX!

For a crypto exchange, the crypto accounting software needs extremely high volume transaction capabilities, with separate parts for tracking customer assets and the company's assets, and deeply granular reporting for auditing and compliance with accounting standards.

Whether it's a start-up crypto exchange or an e-commerce operation accepting crypto payments - systemising this in Excel is a wildly inefficient and error-prone way to handle digital asset transactions. When our clients are open to using a robust and scalable ERP system then it's a joy!

We've helped to onboard clients to several different crypto subledger accounting systems, reviewed others, and found that their niche functionalities vary. Which crypto accounting tools have you used and what were your biggest implementation challenges?

Silly Facebook won't let me share the article. Cryptocurrency payments come to Adelaide Oval in Victoria! Rob Green thanks for letting me know about this. 

I thought it was interesting because there has been a bit of hoo-hah protest over cash not being accepted at these events anymore... so now they can pay with e-cash instead! 

So - who knows the rules on personal use CGT exemption and how it applies to paying for things with crypto? Need a separate wallet that's freshly funded for private spending, to avoid CGT. So ask your clients if they maintain a wallet that's only for private spending and wasn't funded from an investment wallet transfer, generally speaking. 

Would you buy a beer and pie with your crypto wallet?

The task of integrating crypto transactions into standard accounting systems has been a persistent challenge for accountants, Web3 start-ups and crypto company CFOs alike. At Electra Frost Accounting my original practice we first encountered Bitcoin in 2013, with an animated film production client who was paying overseas artists with BTC. In these early days prior to any official tax and accounting guidance, we exercised professional judgment to apply FOREX accounting. We were excited by this innovative new form of money and curiously prepared complex Excel spreadsheets with currency conversions to post journals into Xero. Crypto accounting subledger tools sit between the blockchain and your accounting system. They import transaction data directly from wallets and exchanges, apply accounting rules and cost basis methods, generate journals, and push reconciled entries into Xero, QuickBooks, or NetSuite. This eliminates the manual CSV work and reduces the error rate significantly. The key distinction from crypto tax software is that subledger tools maintain the double-entry integrity required for proper financial statements - not just tax calculations. For a Web3 start-up or a business with significant crypto activity, this is the difference between financial reporting that can be audited and a spreadsheet that might pass a basic tax return.
title: Automating Your Way Out of Manual Crypto Accounting

**What is the true valuation of Bitcoin? **

Ever find yourself in a debate about Bitcoin's worth with friends or family who are yet to study crypto world? They often mention financial experts who claim, "Bitcoin's value is merely what someone else is willing to pay." But as accountants we know there's more to measuring value.

Beyond this simplified view, multiple models can offer insight into Bitcoin's valuation:

1. **Stock to Flow Model:** This considers Bitcoin's available supply against its annual production. Historically, it's been a solid indicator of Bitcoin's price trends. It looks at two attributes: stock (circulating supply) and flow (new supply). (see image below)
1. **Comparables:** Similar to how we assess real estate by comparing it with similar properties, Bitcoin can be evaluated against the price of other similar assets to gauge its worth.
1. **Metcalfeâs Law:** The value of a network, like Bitcoin, grows exponentially with each new user, increasing its potential transactions and overall worth, especially now with added network effects of Layer 2s (anchored to Bitcoin)
1. **Total Addressable Market (TAM):** Bitcoin is often compared to gold, with its market potential pegged at around $14.5 trillion. So if everyone swapped their gold for Bitcoin...
1. **Cost of Production:** Research shows Bitcoin's price tends to align with its production costs, though this doesn't account for its market demand. Commodities don't trade at their production cost.
1. **Revenue Multiples:** The Bitcoin network generates significant revenues for miners who secure it with their computing power.  Today miners made ~$75MM in revenues. For context, Nvidia's valuation is at 35x its sales.

Does this cover all the valuation methods, or is there a model you believe paints a better picture of Bitcoin's value?

During a recent Institute of Professional Accountants Town Hall Webinar, it was revealed that many professional accountants are not fulfilling their CPE/CPD requirements due to lack of time and cost of training. I proposed publicly displaying CPE/CPD achievements and logged hours to encourage compliance and prioritise continuous learning. By leveraging public blockchain technologies, accountants can establish trust, prove their value, and ensure transparency and integrity in the profession. What I am describing is CREDU - a decentralised credentialling system for accountants built on Bitcoin and open blockchain protocols. CREDU would allow practitioners to log their CPD activities on-chain, have them verified by peers and professional bodies, and display them publicly in a way that is tamper-proof and portable. The credential would belong to the practitioner, not the institution that awarded it. This is a significant departure from current CPD systems, which are institution-controlled, not portable, and not publicly verifiable. CREDU would also change the economics of CPD by enabling a value-for-value model in which practitioners pay for content with attention and contribution rather than subscription fees.
title: Breaking New Ground in Accounting CPE & CPD with a Decentralised Application - Part 1

#Ordinals embed data onto individual Satoshis, the smallest unit of Bitcoin, using the BRC20 protocol. This allows for unique tokens with digital content attached, creating non-fungible digital collectibles on the Bitcoin blockchain.


hashtag
#Stamps, on the other h&, leverage the SRC20 protocol, embedding data directly into the UTXO set. This ensures permanence within the blockchain & introduces semi-fungibility, allowing users to issue different types of digital assets.

3/ 🔐 Security & Immutability


hashtag
#Ordinals face potential data pruning, impacting long-term security. The BRC20 protocol's reliance on nodes raises concerns about data loss & alteration, as the decentralized network may prune old witness data.


hashtag
#Stamps, by embedding data in the UTXO set, offer enhanced security & immutability. This approach ensures that once data is on the blockchain, it remains permanently preserved, immune to pruning or manual alterations.

4/ 🚀 Use Cases


hashtag
#Ordinals are suitable for applications where data permanence is not critical. The potential for data pruning may limit their use in scenarios requiring enduring data integrity.


hashtag
#Stamps shine in applications demanding enduring data integrity. Legal documents, certificates, & historical records benefit from the immutability of Stamps, ensuring long-term reliability.

5/ ⚖️ Regulatory Compliance

The potential for data loss & alterations in 
hashtag
#Ordinals may raise regulatory compliance concerns, especially in industries requiring tamper-proof records.


hashtag
#Stamps, with their permanence, provide a robust solution for regulatory compliance. The tamper-proof nature aligns with legal standards, making them a reliable choice in industries where data integrity is paramount.

6/ 📊 Scalability

The Ordinalq BRC20 protocol, relying on nodes to store witness data, may contribute to blockchain bloat, potentially impacting 
hashtag
#scalability. Scalability concerns arise as adoption grows.


hashtag
#Stamps, designed to occupy minimal space in the UTXO set, contribute to efficient scalability. This minimizes the impact on the overall efficiency of the Bitcoin network, addressing concerns related to blockchain bloat.

7/ ✨ Potential for Innovation

The 
hashtag
#Ordinals/BRC20 protocol offers an innovative way to tag & track individual 
hashtag
#Satoshis, providing granularity in data embedding. However, the potential for data pruning introduces a trade-off in terms of permanence. 
hashtag
#Innovation


hashtag
#Stamps bring innovation with semi-fungibility, allowing users to issue various types of digital assets. The direct embedding into the UTXO set ensures a secure & lasting record of ownership, fostering new possibilities.

A proposed merger of Australia's three major accounting bodies signals a move towards further centralisation, a trend viewed as outdated and futile by leading technologists in the digital economy. A more democratic and innovative approach is emerging: forming a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation. A DAO is a significant departure from traditional unions and mergers. Rather than amalgamating Australia's three major professional accounting bodies into a single centralised entity, a DAO would merge the members of these bodies into a member-governed digital organisation. This approach includes the existing professional bodies in a non-hierarchical DAO structure, without replacing or controlling them. The DAO model enables accountants to retain their professional designations and institutional affiliations while participating in a new layer of decentralised governance that gives members more direct influence over the standards, education, and advocacy that affect their practices. The merger proposal treats centralisation as the solution to fragmentation. The DAO model treats decentralisation as the solution to both fragmentation and the unresponsiveness that comes with centralised institutional control. If we are going to create a dominant structure for the accounting profession, it should be a structure that its members control directly.
title: DAO vs Merger: Merge the Accountants, not their Accounting Bodies

text:
My first LinkedIn article! Instead of merging Australia's 3 major accounting bodies, why don't we merge the members in a digital organisation? If we're creating a monopoly, make it a decentralised monopoly! 

It's a perfect context to consider a Decentralised Autonomous Organisation (DAO) for governance and directing more value to members.

A digital organisation on a network model is destined to be a monopoly, and (referencing Paul Brody from EY, 'Ethereum for Business') if we are going to build business models on a monopoly network, the most public, permissionless, secure and decentralised monopoly is the only kind worth having. The necessary functionality may now be achieved through the use of layered solutions, protocols, and proposals built on top of the Bitcoin network.

What do you think? 🤓

Financial advisers cannot recommend 
hashtag
#bitcoin because, like crypto, it is not classified as a financial product. As such, it isn't covered by their professional indemnity insurance policies and that precludes recommendations in an advice document. A financial adviser might discuss pros and cons of Bitcoin with their clients but will avoid making recommendations. Ask them what they've read lately to see if they're curious and keeping up to date.

ð Tune in at 6.30pm AEST tonight for the followup from SBAABTA DAO education webinar. Talking about the real purpose of a Digital Organisation built on the values and community spirit of this group and the most cutting edge technologies to solve some of the problems of our professional bodies that we, as members, complain about.  We'll talk about:

1ï¸â£ Joe Kaleb's post on LinkedIn about merging the 3 accounting bodies.

A merger is a further centralisation and with potential for monopolisation. Whereas, a decentralised model led by members is the opposite but tackles the same problems that a merger means to solve.

A DAO would be a merging of the 3 accounting bodies MEMBERS, not the 3 accounting bodies themselves. 

A DAO would INCLUDE the professional bodies and associations in a completely member-governed digital organisation. So it doesn't replace them, just gives us more influence over them.

---------------------------------------------

2ï¸â£ What does SBAABTA (this group) do for you that your professional body doesn't do.

So many strong comments in that thread remind us that if a new Digital Organisation that decentralises our professional associations is based on this group, then it mustn't destroy a beautiful thing. How can it enhance it?

---------------------------------------------

3ï¸â£ "AI will replace all accountants except for the small number employed by Big4" - the argument given by the start-up accelerator that rejected my project for global accountants.

I don't think we should dismiss this out of hand, even if they were fools with no idea about accounting and probably the next FTX.

We are stronger in numbers and can use technology to organise ourselves and harness our own content for AI benefits that flow to us, rather than to the top, so we can keep supporting the small business economy.

---------------------------------------------

4ï¸â£ Mark Newman's post about the world getting so complex that we need to stick to our areas of expertise, for our wellbeing. 

By the same token, we have to collaborate to remain competitive. Large accounting companies with vast resources of human expertise, BI and AI are eyeing our target markets.

Collectively, we need to compete with them as a highly effective network of niches. So that when you get a client with a winery, you and your AI agent can tap into the WET expertise of John Smith CPA and keep that client.

Freelance economies like this can be created in blockchain based digital organisations, such as a DAO. They are completely member-run - they don't have a central authority such as a franchisor or accounting body. They are decentralised and autonomous, running on smart contracts to distribute value and work amongst its members.
So, yes, business is getting a whole lot harder but if we prioritise our time now to adapting to the RIGHT "future of work" then we can make things better in the next 10 years.

---------------------------------------------

and finally 

5ï¸â£ ETHICS ... who decides where the lines are drawn? We do!

This week members disagreed on how an employee who faked their covid jab certificate should be treated, in particular the ethical ramifications of it. 

DAO voting mechanisms allow members to shape governance of ethical standards within a DAO. It's healthy to respectfully share different views (which this group is so good at!)... but rather than asserting fixed views on ethics and saying we are appalled by each other we could could just anonymously vote.... on a very large scale! 

How that could be done, by design, it just one of the many jobs of the experts in the establishment team of a DAO. So we will look at how a professional services DAO is structured to enact governance.

---------------------------------------------

oh and 6ï¸â£ 

The vision of monetised peer to peer CPD. How we get paid by each other for supporting each other in a Value 4 Value model?

---------------------------------------------

So that'll be me talking for 40-45 mins
With 15-20 mins Q&A's in between topics
It'll be recorded and shared publicly.

Come along and listen if you're curious about the future of digital accounting practice and might want to play a role in shaping this. I'm not a leader, just a facilitator and researcher. Your ideas are important. 

Oh and if that merger pushes ahead ... maybe we'll ask IPA/CA/CPA to consider a DAO instead

I hosted an Accountants On-Chain online event prompted by a member post: "since we get more information and support from this group than CPA, IPA, CA - can we set up our own body to compete!" I replied: we can set up a DAO. It won't cost us anything if we contribute know-how and support in a Value 4 Value model. A DAO for accountants would not replace professional bodies. It would give members more direct influence over the profession. It would be member-governed, member-funded, and member-run. It would enable practitioners to collaborate on standards, share knowledge, and advocate collectively without going through institutional gatekeepers. The knowledge that currently lives in private Facebook groups and Slack channels would be on-chain, attributed, and rewarded. The CPD that currently requires expensive subscriptions would be peer-validated and publicly verifiable. The governance that currently happens in board rooms would happen in open votes. This is not a utopian vision. It is a practical application of tools that already exist to a problem that already affects every small practice accountant in Australia.

The inclination of digital-native consumers is reshaping the expectations around business transactions. These consumers are anticipated to prefer transacting in cryptocurrencies, valuing speed, privacy, and convenience. Australian SMEs and digital businesses will eventually need to adapt their payment systems to accommodate crypto transactions. To understand the crypto space we need to become familiar with new terminology and concepts, just as we did when we first learned to surf the web. ElectraFi expects all currencies to become digital this decade. We need to grasp the difference between centralised and decentralised digital currencies, the applications of blockchain beyond currency, and the regulatory and tax environment that is rapidly developing around them. The key message for accountants: do not wait for clarity before engaging. The clients who most need your help navigating this space are already in it. The accountants who are learning now will be the ones they call.
title: Cryptocurrency — the challenges, the opportunities… and what it really means for your business or accounting practice

I decided to join Blockchain Australia after finding that its Members' Code of Ethical Conduct resonates well with my professional accounting ethics. As a Fellow member of the Institute of Public Accountants, I am bound to abide by principles of professional ethics contained in the IPA's By-laws and APES 110 Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants. As a Registered Tax Practitioner, I must also observe the TASA's Code of Professional Conduct. Having operated a professional accounting practice for more than 15 years, I recently pivoted my practice to focus on Bitcoin and blockchain business and accounting. The alignment between the accounting profession's ethical framework and the blockchain industry's values of transparency, accountability, and trustless verification is not coincidental. Both are fundamentally about maintaining integrity in the recording and reporting of value. The accounting profession and the Bitcoin industry share a core commitment: that the integrity of records matters, that verification should be possible, and that trust should be earned through transparency rather than assumed through authority.
title: Accountants, Bitcoin and Blockchain Industry: Aligned by Ethical Principles

text: I'm a professional accountant mapping the latest maze of Bitcoin, crypto, DeFi, Web3 and blockchain rabbit holes for accountants to confidently navigate. I care strongly about future-proofing my profession and the small businesses that we advocate for, so I'm always looking for the really empowering technology that can make us all more profitable and resilient in the long-term. I've owned an accounting practice for more than 15 years and was an early adopter of digital business technologies. We first put Bitcoin on the balance sheet in 2014 and things have been getting stranger and more exciting ever since. In 2022, I led the Accountants On-Chain knowledge-sharing project with Digital Playhouse Foundation, the charity I co-founded. My revamped accounting practice, ElectraFi, is now continuing that project as a social enterprise and offering helpful services to accountants with crypto business clients.

I've inscribed the www.electrafi.finance company logo as an Ordinal NFT. An Ordinal is like an NFT you can mint directly onto the Bitcoin blockchain, as a complete digital asset file (not a digital representation) embedded within a satoshi. Your Ordinal can be held in a Bitcoin wallet, or transferred using a Bitcoin transaction.

Back in 2012, âcoloured coinsâ was a first approach to add extra data to individual satoshis, representing real world assets on the Bitcoin blockchain, thus was analogous of âcolouringâ them. Due to their limitations, NFTs were invented which instead reside on Ethereum, Solana and other protocols.

This changed with Taproot addresses (start with bc1P) which offer novel smart contract opportunities for Bitcoin addresses, compared to Bitcoin addresses such as Legacy or P2PKH (start with the number 1), P2SH (start with the number 3) and Segwit/Bech32 (start with bc1).

You can use ordinal blockchain explorers such as [https://ordinals.hiro.so/](https://ordinals.hiro.so/) to search for an inscription with a number of criteria; in this case: inscription #29762337

The Bitcoin community has been somewhat divided on whether NFTs on its blockchain are a good thing. While many see them as a positive way to further grow its ecosystem, some âBitcoin maximalistsâ oppose them for taking up block space on the network and making transaction fees more expensive; some hold concerns about causing non-fungibility and tracking of sats.

I appreciate that uploading non-financial data that will be forever included on the Bitcoin blockchain should not be a frivolous undertaking. Ordinal theory is a methodology for individually identifying (with a serial number) and tracking each individual satoshi throughout the Bitcoin coin supply. With around 2 quadrillion sats, very few of these sats have have been inscribed so far. If all the sats had inscriptions, you wouldn't be able to use Bitcoin like cash anymore, right?

While I agree that this isn't the place for monkey JPEG's and vanity projects, I think that immutably storing valuable property and identity data has merit. And with the advent of recursive inscriptions taking ordinals inscriptions to the next level, with complex data sets of audio and video files hosted on Bitcoin, we could be building "websites" on the most immutable, decentralised and censorship-resistant network in the world.

So my humble company logo is just going to lay in wait to see what becomes possible. And if I sell this business or turn it into a DAO, the Ordinal inscription is an included asset.

Some days in Bitcoin I feel like I'm back in 1996, sitting in my boyfriend's attic building a website on his Macintosh... hacking with reckless abandon!

Bitcoin's fusion of ancient monetary principles with cutting edge technology, is challenging our notion of what constitutes 'real money'. 

Beyond money, Bitcoin's scalability is ensured as a settlement layer for other economic activities on Layer 2's and sidechains, and through merging with the renewable energy sector. It's not only the world's most widely accepted currency after the US dollar, it also provides a global economic system prioritising security and reliability... for anyone to use... with no CEO, no bank, no controllers... just energy and maths. 

All accountants need to become conversant on these topics. The reason I got my head around blockchain and web3 quickly and understood what makes it valuable is because I studied Bitcoin first. Start with Bitcoin, and the rest falls into place!

Can dividends be taken as any kind of property instead of as cash? I believe so but can't find anything to back me up in the tax act and can't remember dealing with this before.

 I have a client who wants to take all his USDC stablecoin out of his company for his big annual dividend, and he doesn't want to off ramp to cash... crypto is property under the tax act...  and this has actually stumped me!

ð

note:
- definition of 'dividend' in subsection 6(1) of the ITAA 1936 has the general effect that any distribution made by a company to any of its shareholders, whether in money or property, and any amount credited by a company to any of its shareholders as shareholders, is a dividend.

but paragraph d) excludes distributions that were debited against an amount credited on the company's share capital account... I don't think that applies here as it's just company retained income reserves parked into USDC

Yet so many business owners think accountants are too expensive and try to manage things themselves all year round... or use a basic bookkeeping service... only seeing their tax agent once a year to rustle together a tax return! 

We've already prepared everyones' tax returns for year end 30 June 2023. All our clients' obligations are planned in advance based on real time data and forecasts, no surprises, everything managed and paid behind the scenes. 

Business owners have no idea what they're missing by not having monthly reporting on performance, budget variances and balances until the cash flow crisis and director penalties bite. 

Three quarters of small business owners stated that, if starting up again, implementating a robust financial management system would be a top priority. Half regretted not engaging an accountant and bookkeeper up front. It's no surprise, really. Running a profitable, legal business is really hard.

If you know a business earning $500K upwards, how we do things differently can make a huge difference to their 1-5 year outcomes and selling price... Please point them my way, I'll demonstrate what full scope accounting services can do for them.

Ernst & Young has built and launched their own decentralised, permissionless Layer 2 (L2) over the public Ethereum Network. 

E&Y's blockchain strategy sends a powerful message to our industry about private/permissioned blockchains - indicating that they're on the wrong side of the fundamental innovation brought forth by *public blockchains.*

Per Paul Brody, E&Y's head of Global blockchain: "we don't see the value proposition in permissioned systems that claim to be decentralized."

If you're not aware, E&Y is the only Big 4 company solely focused on the *public* Ethereum network.

They turn down consulting engagements with anyone building on *private/permissioned* blockchains. Why? Probably because they view it as a short-term money grab.

---

Back to the L2 launch.

In the opinion of Michael Nadeu (the DeFi report), an opinion I share:

 "the existence of *private/permissioned* blockchains is due to the shortcomings of public blockchains *to date.* In particular, scalability and privacy.

"L2s are beginning to solve scalability constraints by batching transactions off-chain, and then recording proofs in bundled transactions to the L1 for final settlement.

"[This is the innovation of Ethereum: a credibly neutral, shared, permissionless global settlement network & database. It's a mouthful. But it's what allows businesses and individuals to engage with each other, and record their economic activity in a shared, neutral database without trusting each other.]

"Privacy is solved by zero-knowledge proofs: the ability to prove that something happened, without revealing the contents of the data."

----

"In essence, this could open the floodgates for enterprises to utilise public networks like Ethereum [ added - and Stacks / Bitcoin] .

E&Y is working on several manufacturing/industrial supply chain use cases.

They forecast 4 billion transactions/day for this use case alone.

And they think that 50% of enterprises will be on *public blockchains* within 10 years â in line with the timeline it took for enterprises to migrate to the cloud."

----

"Crypto" gets dragged through the mud quite a bit (and rightly so).

But in an interesting twist of irony, the ultimate blockchain crime could ultimately be revealed as "private/permissioned blockchain."

Or maybe we end up somewhere in the middle?

What do you think?

The uptake of bitcoin by businesses globally is not insignificant, and accountants are noticing it's more than an investment. Read Damian Lloyd's paper to learn the history and technical terms of Bitcoin, and how it functions as borderless 'money', as a means of payment and for savings. 

Did you know that bitcoin was adopted as the legal tender of El Salvador in 2021? However, Australian legislation does not recognise Bitcoin as currency, which places a significant administrative burden on taxpayers who use it as a mean of exchange, personally and in business. 

The unspent transaction outputs (UTXOs) on the distributed Bitcoin blockchain satisfy the meaning of 'foreign currency' and should be accounted for as such, as argued in the following paper.

Learn blockchain and web3 with deeper insights by starting with Bitcoin.

#tax #bitcoin #POS #smallbusiness This stallholder at my local markets is using the new Wallet of Satoshi POS.  

So I bought coffee and kombucha from him and had a chat about accounting and tax.. 

My Wallet of Satoshi (WoS) is only used for private purchases, so I ensure that only "fresh" purchases of Bitcoin fund that wallet so as to avoid having to account for CGT. i.e. I do not include transactions from that particular wallet in my tax return.

How? Part of my salary is paid through getpaidinbitcoin.com.au, employer pays AUD and it converts to BTC for me, and I direct a portion of that Bitcoin to my Wallet of Satoshi "hot wallet" each fortnight for "spending sats". 

I can also buy bitcoin directly using my WoS but it's not as economical. 

As soon as I transfer any bitcoin from my investing/savings wallets to this WoS, I believe it would end the wallet's private use exemption from CGT. It's important for consumers to know how to manage wallet hygiene to take advantage of this CGT-free treatment of private btc transactions.

From the merchant's POV, he is only using his WoS for business, registered to his business email account and using the POS built into the app. No private transactions should happen in that wallet. 

He will transfer around 30% of his daily or weekly btc takings to his company's Bitaroo exchange account (Bitaroo is Lightning enabled, so it's just a phone to phone scan of a QR code), where he can convert it to AUD and transfer to the tax savings bank account. This makes sure he sets aside an approximate  amount for GST and taxes at the current exchange rate, to not worry about price volatility. 

He is transferring the balance to a secure "cold wallet" (using Ledger or Trezor) to save up bitcoin as an inflation edge for his business. He has documentation of the hardware device being company property (to avoid Director loan Div 7A implications). These savings must be reflected on the balance sheet at cost, not fair value.

To do his accounts, he can export a CSV of the  transactions from his WoS, which will have to be calculated into AUD value to be journal's into his accounting software. 

In the USA, there are USD based services that connect Lightning PoS to Quickbooks. I'm not sure how to automate the process for an Australian merchant using Xero. How would you handle this?

Lightning Network for Bitcoin is 1000x cheaper.
And it can support more than 10x the transaction volume of credit card networks. The future of real time micropayments is on crypto and blockchain - decentralised, global, permissionless and private.
Lightning network is 1000x cheaper and can handle 10x the transaction volume for micropayments. It's just a matter of time for businesses to switch... what will be the tipping point?

The problem with enterprise blockchain, is it did not start with the new incentive model for the internet as part of a new digital economy.

This incentive model is based around the original and rightful owner controlling their pseudo-anonymous reputation and value used as a currency in an atomic swap or trading exchange of an NFT (or FT) representing ownership in an asset born on the internet or with an abstraction layer to the real world. 

In this way value and ownership are transferred at the same time increasing the velocity of money. These exchanges can also be made for the cost of an email eliminating the 2% rent taken by traditional payment rails. 

This grows the size of the digital economy and supports micro transactions need to reward sharing of data (eg from IoT) to drive AI apps. This is the role of Autonomous Economic Agents (AEAâs)

Anti-crypto is anti-competitive. Competition leads to lower prices, more choice and better products for consumers. Those innovating with crypto and blockchain use cases are driving greater value and protections to us than ever before, financially and personally. 

Examples of anti-competitive behaviour include cartel conduct, anti-competitive agreements, exclusionary provisions (boycotts), misuse of market power and exclusive dealings. In Australia, we ban business behaviours that damage competition.

 The Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (CCA) prohibits conduct that is likely to substantially lessen competition in a market. But do anti-crypto operators know they're being anti-competitive?

So I think it's time we aggressively challenged all banks and corporations that discriminate against crypto businesses and users ...and bring to account any regulators and influential politicians who either appear to speak for them or should be educating themselves now (by doing accredited Diplomas in Applied Blockchain, for example).

Any accountant or auditor can be part of this decentralised network to create trust and transparency in the world's financial information... by staking 5000AUDT tokens to secure the network and by operating a Pacioli node (link below inviting beta testers)... and receive royalties for any accounting rules and business intelligence they contribute  (process-control NFTs).

What is money? Money is technology. It's information on a database, with currency as monetary value assigned to the data. Fiat currency's value is based on IOU's, backed only by our trust in the government that issues it. 

Whereas Bitcoin's superior value proposition is absolute mathematical scarcity, achieved by consensus in a sufficiently decentralised network... no trust required. 

Revolutionary concepts of hard money challenge every professional person in finance. But we need to spend time learning how Bitcoin works if we are to maintain our intellectual credibility and integrity this decade. Laughing and dismissing Bitcoin risks one appearing foolish. 

Read as much as you possibly can so we can constructively debate what's in our own and others' best interests. I recommend books like The Bitcoin Standard and The Price of Tomorrow, for the economics. But understanding a bit about the technology of decentralisation is equally important.

But if I misplace the Ledger device I have a spare on-hand to be up and running again quickly.

Here's the Digital Playhouse affiliate link https://coinstop.io/?aff=36
if you'd like to pick some up for yourself and staff. Yes... staff! Ours are getting a feel for the decentralised digital economy too. ð¤

Coinstop is a local distributor we know and trust. Otherwise, always get your device direct from the manufacturer for security reasons.

FYI 
Owning crypto-assets means managing your private keys. The 12-24 word seed phrase gives you exclusive access to your private key. If youâre not owning (i.e. exclusively accessing) your private keys, you donât really own your crypto. By using a Ledger, Trezor or other trusted hardware device sold by the manufacturer, youâre always in complete control of your private keys, meaning that you can recover your funds & assets at any moment if needed, independently of any third party, including the manufacturer of the device.

Jorge, the plantation owner, talked about how farmers' land has been lost to the banks when they've been underpaid for their produce. So to make his plantation more resilient, he decided to learn biodynamics and permaculture, to sell direct to cafes instead of to the mills, to accept bitcoin and start exporting... 

"We did this to stop borrowing from banks, to pay our workers fairly, be proud of what we grow. This is how we can scale ... we are showing the other farms ... now we sell direct to cafes... no intermediaries, saving money .... the mill trucks have stopped coming as we have nothing for them"

El Salvador adopting bitcoin as legal tender and making the country safe from crime has provided a spark of opportunity.
Jorge wants to use a blockchain business model for supply chain provenance, so businesses and individuals around the world know that the source of their coffee is genuine... ethically and sustainably grown, and of the highest quality (not the chaff). The proof is on the blockchain. 

This entrepreneurial surfacing of tech-for-good... this is how small producers could compete on a level playing field with 'big food' and empower their rural communities, pay fruitpickers well etc.

My family were fruit & veg farmers and Jorge's spirit gives me hope for growers in rural Australia who are as curious to learn.

It also motivates me to keep being an accountant to help businesses and lead other accountants to learn Bitcoin.

A common question I'm asked lately is "Do you still want to specialise in crypto and blockchain after everything that's happened with FTX?

My answer is always the same: "Absolutely."

Why?

To me, nothing has changed. Bitcoin is completely unaffected, other than its fiat-denominated price. New blocks continue to be added to the blockchain every 10 minutes. New users keep joining the network. The code is unchanged. There's no CEO, board of directors, or C-suite to defraud anyone.

Bitcoin's running *exactly* as it was designed to, and has done so since 2009. In a society where 8 billion people sit on their hands every couple of months to wait and hear what an old, unelected man in a suit dictates the cost of money will be, Bitcoin is certainty and stability in a world full of unknowns.

While I'm sad to see many losing their jobs in the industry, I'm as bullish as ever for the future of bitcoin. I'm excited to look for my new clients and accounting partners who'll let me continue my mission to support enterprises that will decentralise and democratise finance.

Last month I visited El Salvador to research #bitcoin adoption, focusing on regional traders, entrepreneurship and education.

Last year when the El Salvador government made Bitcoin legal tender, they issued a purpose-built Chivo Wallet, a phone app for Bitcoin Lightning payments, with USD$30 worth of Bitcoin granted to every citizen who downloaded the wallet. 

But there wasn't much public education with this Chivo wallet rollout, I found out. So adoption has been slow particularly in non-urban areas where the largely unbanked population use paper USDs and don't understand or trust digital money yet.

Bitcoin circular economies are created when a trader's customers and suppliers are spending/receiving bitcoin. This is being achieved in tourism areas. If a trader's suppliers only accept dollars, then that trader will want their customers to pay in dollars. But what if the customer only wants to pay bitcoin? They may shop elsewhere... ð

So accepting bitcoin is a competitive advantage for traders in locations where people are getting paid in bitcoin and want to spend it (like me in Agnes Water/1770 Australia... but that's my next post)

Some bitcoin advocates encourage El Salvadorans to use Lightning wallets other than the government one. But a Chivo wallet advantage is it enables you to exchange your Bitcoin for USD on the app... 

This in-app exchange facility is great for traders managing cash flow ...they can avoid price volatility and pay their business expenses in USD ...while leaving a balance in bitcoin as savings, protected from currency debasement, to grow their wealth (in a separate cold wallet, ideally). 

Also, the local bitcoin ATMs let them scan their Lightning wallets and withdraw US dollar notes. 

So, with the available tooling, there is no real obstacle to businesses adopting bitcoin other than education. 

Do you know?:
Which Lightning payment apps, other than Chivo, can a trader use to accept bitcoin payments, that have a built-in exchange-to-fiat facility?

What is Digital 3? (RMIT University media release)
RMIT has officially launched its flagship digital economy business school ‘Digital3’ which incorporates world leading research centres including the Blockchain Innovation Hub. 

Blockchain Innovation Hub Co Director, Jason Potts, provided a keynote address at the launch which encapsulated why this model would be critical to the economy in the current tech revolution. 

“This revolution is due to a supercluster of digital tech, sometimes called web3, but it's broader than that.… it is the refactoring and upgrade of the economic operating system.” Read Jason’s complete speech here.
 
First D3 course dropped & available to study FREE online
As part of the launch, Digital3 (D3) dropped an exciting and ‘first of its kind’ web3 business course called ‘Doing Business in Web3’ which is now available to study online at no cost.*

With a duration of six weeks, the online course takes participants through a carefully crafted journey covering diverse topics including strategy, governance, law, token economics and scaling. 

Course developer and Blockchain Innovation Hub Researcher Dr Darcy Allen, says that the course curates the knowledge needed to build business models in web3. “The web3 stack — including blockchains and smart contracts — fundamentally changes how we do business in a digital economy. This course helps to fill that gap in web3 business education.”

The 'Doing Business in web3' course is now available through FutureLearn with a continuous start date so new participants can join and start learning at any time

D3 will continue to roll out online courses and you can register your interest in future courses scheduled to be dropped here."

#directorsID #decentralisedID #KYC
This is about understanding some of the fringe clients active in the crypto space. There are various freedom movement networks on Telegram warning people about the compulsory Director ID registration required by the ATO. 

Do you get clients asking you if there is any way of avoiding or working around Directors IDs? 

These are my opinions and I'd like to know yours.

It's easy to flip them off as silly or conspiracy theorists ...but if we're educating our clients to be successful in the evolving digital economy, I think we need to be respectful.... and tackle this head on ... encourage more pragmatic and competitive thinking.

We get that they're concerned about government digital overreach etc etc.... but these freedom movements only seem to be fear mongering and leading people into trouble. Not offering new knowledge or strategic options other than "just say no". Like the cash brigade.

"the more you resist, the more it persists"

They're better off learning about decentralised ID and getting behind those projects than trying to fight Director IDs. Learn about the democratising web3 technology being developed that will allow us to legally interact with corporate and government platforms while owning and protecting their personal and business data. 

Thoughts?

Who'd like a session on decentralised identity?

BoT Review of taxation of digital assets
Has anyone been following the submissions? Please share. Time Wang, the co-founder of coinstash.com.au wrote on LinkedIn - wanting to see staking rewards and airdrops income be capitalised and GST rollover reliefs... sounds good... not sure what he means about the GST treatment with regard to DCE's though:

"For those of us that trade sometimes we come across situations where the tax outcome seems highly unpractical or even ridiculous. For example, if you were exposed to Luna/UST, chances are your tax bill is now bigger than your entire crypto portfolio.

The good news? The Board of Taxation is currently doing a review of treatment of digital assets and transactions. Coinstash is happy to put forward our view on three key areas:

1) Treat certain staking rewards and airdrops as capital in nature;
2) the CGT roll-over reliefs should be expanded to crypto assets; and
3) Application of the correct GST treatment by all registered DCEs.

I look forward to reading the final report presented by the Board of Taxation to the government."

Consultation on the Board of Taxation Review of the Tax Treatment of Digital Assets and Transactions closes on Friday 30 September 2022.

This is your chance to have a say in what could result in one of the most significant changes to Australian tax law since GST.

There are significant issues with the taxation of digital assets and transactions under current Australian tax law and if Australia is to be a leader in the blockchain and innovation space in the future, the issues must be addressed as a matter of urgency.

If you think this only applies to individuals who dabble in cryptocurrency you are wrong - blockchain is the future, it will impact on entity types, decision making models, income tax, GST, international tax, transfer pricing, business products...

Be a part of the change.

In the 1980's Warren Buffet identified that a business's intangible assets increase with inflation: “What is more likely is that the Goodwill will increase – in current, if not in constant, dollars – because of inflation". 

At today's Accountants Academy induction workshop we talked about Balance Sheet Thinking...beyond the hard assets, all the intangibles that are not shown on the balance sheet...

 That includes all your business information, processes and innovations that ought to be documented and fully systemised across the organisation. To generate profits and scale upon. 

Now, I realise Mr Buffet isn't a fan of Bitcoin technology... but he may change his mind when incumbent firms are creating NFTs from all this valuable business information that they can securely own in blockchain and that others will pay good crypto to use, in a completely automated Web3 marketplace.

Complete information proprietorship monetised in Web3. That'll be the way I scale.

Ok, we may not know how to do that yet. 
But we can write down all our processes now, create a register, so it's all ready to go. Small steps are the way to big goals

We are pretty sure that staking rewards are taxable income (but not quite certain yet) - but a question that is never answered no matter how much I Google it is...

*WHEN* are staking rewards taxed. The derivation point is much much more complex than it appears. Questions I'll think about all weekend:

Are they taxed when they appear in your wallet?

Are they taxed when you can claim them via a smart contract call?

Is the taxing point when you can actually access them? (think Beacon chain ETH staking)

Does cash and accruals accounting methods impact this?

Does the constructive receipt rule in section 6-5(4) bring forward the derivation time?

Just like when we analysed Anchor, you have to get into the guts of a protocol to understand the tax outcomes. Generalisations at this stage of the crypto tax evolution can very easily lead to bad advice. Also the case law on derivation is scant and fact specific, we are mostly on our own on this one.

The digital economy: 3 things 7-figure 
hashtag
#businessowners know…

That 5-figure business owners still haven’t caught onto yet.

Keep reading to find out more:

Traditional 
hashtag
#banking rails shrink your marketplace: Paying or receiving funds from the western world is pretty easy. The banking connections are set, margins are thin and we are all rightly or wrongly locked in and benefitting from the race to the bottom. 

But for those looking to add value in underserviced parts of the world the game is different. It takes real buying power, a long list of middlemen and local know-how to jump through the long list of 
hashtag
#regulatory and 
hashtag
#custom hoops and still successfully turn a profit. Those that do can then enjoy the wider margins as it is a privilege reserved for the few.

Traditional banking rails are to slow: Even with SWIFT and corresponding banks, businesses are forced to accept 2-3 working days for funds to become available. This bottleneck in cash flow poses numerous risks but the main one is a scary thought. Imagine if your direct competitors could get access to their funds 25% quicker than you could, consistently. How long would it take them to maximise a competitive advantage? How much more demand could they create? How much more inventory could they take on and respond to that demand faster?

Suppliers make concessions to those who make it easier: Businesses in emerging markets have switched to crypto. This trend continues to grow as it allows them to level the playing field in an ultra-competitive global market. Western businesses have started to recognise this. They decide to pay said suppliers in crypto to make their lives easier in exchange for a discount on the services provided. Win/Win.


So to recap, this is what you need to remember:

A standard currency account will not carry you far in the digital world. Open your businesses up to blockchain, make the most of the speed and efficiency it can provide and let your team sell into a broader market.

To play globally you have to think outside the box: traditional banking rails stop this.
Speed kills: Cashflow is king and whilst it is stuck in the pipes you cannot put it to work.
Suppliers/clients will do business with whoever makes it easier: 
hashtag
#Crypto is becoming more and more popular in emerging markets, you can no longer rely on just a traditional bank account.

Bitcoin is capped at 21 million units so how can it replace money? It's not enough for billions of people. Correct! A monetary system must accommodate global demand for currency units. Hence, the #SAT 

The #fiat solution: create more fiat units --> results in a loss of purchasing power until the currency collapses. By design, inflation is guaranteed, what is variable is the rate of inflation, the Consumer Price Index (CPI.) The CPI is calculated by the increasing or decreasing prices of the government-selected (btw, continuously changing) consumer basket of goods to determine the official inflation number. The government aims at debasing the money at a 2% rate. Why 2%? Because at this rate of debasement (inflation), people don't protest on the streets, and politicians can still get elected or re-elected. With fiat, saving is a losing proposition that necessitates making investments (inherently risky) in an attempt to outpace inflation.

The #bitcoin proposition: create a fixed number of SATs when more SATs are needed then divide them --> resulting in a gain of purchasing power, forever! Once you earn your money, your bitcoin, you can store it for the future knowing that it will give you more purchasing power. Today, since 98% of the world still has NOT discovered the power of bitcoin, it is necessary to hold it for 200 Weeks. Meaning, hold your bitcoin for 3.8 years and you will have added to your net worth. With bitcoin, saving is a winning proposition where there's no need to make any investments.

Don't get confused.

The total number of SATS will never change, there will only be 20999999.97690000 x 100M SATs, PERIOD.

Now, perhaps when the need arises to further subdivide a sat, say in 100 years, 200 years, we could call them #nakomotos or #NAKs where 1 SAT = 100M NAKs. Why 100M NAKs? To honor Satoshi Nakamoto, the inventor of Bitcoin.
