We can best understand our crypto clients from Andy Martin's perspective. Web3 is the internet of value, right, and internet money really is a big deal. ð¤¯

"Sometimes the most important things in life are the most obvious. You see what we are doing with blockchain, now called web3 or âmetaverse as a token economyâ is.... trumpets blaring â¦ creating decentralised, censorship resistant, digital, and programmable money for internet payments, just like what we do today with cash or paper money.

 So why is that a big deal? A super-big deal? Well, there is only three things we need to get right for the new internet:

1.    Who controls your identity on the internet?

2.    Who controls what you own on the internet?

3.    Who controls payments, as that ownership is transferred, between those identities, on the internet?

And your identity means: a human, a machine, or a company. As we move into the hyper connected world of the future, your fridge will negotiate with your supermarket and your diary to decide what food you buy.

So are you happy, if on the internet â¦

1.    Your identity is owned by Google, Amazon, Meta or Apple

2.    The police state (George Orwell 1984) becomes possible â we know everything about you

3.    You become a digital serf â because you gave anyway your data for âfreeâ services

I donât want to become a digital serf. Do you? 

So yes, internet money is a big deal. I will be talking about what I think this means to the new digital citizen and the enterprises that serve these new, empowered digital customers."
  
Andy Martin will be talking about what all this means for the incumbent enterprise on https://get.blockchain.ieee.org/regional-events/americas/ on Wed 24th August.

Tax practitioners based in Australia: Here's an easy opportunity to have your say on blockchain and tax compliance!

If you have dealt with at least one client who has undertaken blockchain-related activities (such as traded/invested in cryptoassets etc.), you're invited to have your say by participating in a short 15-minute on-line survey by Dr Elizabeth Morton (RMIT).
https://rmit.au1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_3HSYwy9VHonOdIG?jfefe=new

It has become evident that taxpayers are increasingly participating in blockchain technology. As a result, there has been an increasing interest in the implications for tax compliance, including the impact and challenges of these activities on tax practitioners.

In 2021, the Senate Select Committee on Australia as a Technology and Financial Centre Final Report flagged recommendations concerning Blockchain activities with regards to the CGT implications as well as the need for ATO guidance to be more up to date. Whilst the Government noted the recommendation, it gave the Treasury the task of working with the Board of Taxation to undertake a broader review of the appropriate framework for taxing digital transactions and assets. In March 2022, the Board released its terms of reference for its review to be provided to the Treasurer by the end of the year.

In this regard, research is being undertaken by Dr Elizabeth Morton of RMIT University, as well as Assoc Prof Gillian Vesty (RMIT University), Dr Lan Nguyen (RMIT University) and Assoc Prof Ken Devos (Swinburne University), on the impact and challenges of blockchain-related activities on tax practitioners in Australia. The purpose of this project is to examine the blockchain-related activities with respect to tax compliance.

Current anecdotal evidence suggests that further technical and theoretical skills are required to support blockchain understanding and tax practices. Consequently, the empirical evidence that will be collected through this project will greatly assist in identifying this potential tax practitioner skillâs gap, while also contributing to the consultation processes with government.

Participation in this research project is voluntary and anonymous.

How to complete the survey: You are invited to take part in a short 15-minute survey by clicking on the link below to express your views on the implications of blockchain technology on public practice and tax compliance:
https://rmit.au1.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_3HSYwy9VHonOdIG?jfefe=new

If you would like further information on this project, please contact:
Dr. Elizabeth Morton, co-investigator, +61 3 9925 5886, elizabeth.morton@rmit.edu.au
All correspondence will be treated confidentially.

Bull markets incite greed and bring about opportunities to leverage by borrowing against current crypto assets to buy more crypto. 

There is a lot of leverage in the system.

$900M+ worth of leverage has already been liquidated.

#costofproduction 
Here's one for the #managementaccountants with clients #mining . How much it costs #miners to produce each #Bitcoin in electricity or whatever their energy source of choice is, greatly impacts profit margins. The next major factor is the price each BTC is currently trading at.

When the cost to produce each Bitcoin is higher than the assetâs price, thereâs a higher chance that miners will sell their BTC supply to fund operations or as they cash out and capitulate.

A tool has been developed that measures the current cost of production and layers it over Bitcoin price action. This tool is aptly named the Cost of Production indicator.

From Panagiotis Kriaris (LinkedIn):
The meteoric rise of ecommerce over the past years has profoundly changed not only how consumers shop but also the way businesses are run. Yet, the degree of change so far is only a fraction of what is coming. Letâs take a look.
 
Start with the numbers:

â     There are over 2.14 billion global online shoppers and between 12 to 24 million online shops (according to digital intheround, 2021 figures)

â     The global ecommerce industry is forecast to grow by almost $11 trillion between 2021 and 2025 with an annualized growth rate (CAGR in industry jargon) of almost 29%, according to Report Linker

â     Global ecommerce sales will reach $6.4 trillion by 2024 according to Statista
 
Customers today have at their disposal more ways than ever to discover, engage with and evaluate products and a wider - than ever before - array of digital and physical venues (or their combination) to buy them.
 
At the heart of the disruption so far, we find four fundamental shifts:
 
â    A behavioral shift towards digital, mobile-first experiences, accelerated (but not initiated) by the pandemic 
 
â    A transition from products to experiences with a complete overhaul of the customer journey
 
â    The hyper-personalization and frictionlessness of experiences have taken their place not as a nice-to-have but as a requirement in an omnichannel world where the shift from digital to physical interactions and vice-versa is the new norm
 
â    technology acting as a key innovation enabler and disruption driver
 
Despite the global appeal and application of these four shifts, there are still strong regional and local variations when it comes to payments, the lifeblood of e-commerce: for example, Chinese shoppers use mostly e-wallets (61%) whereas Americans still mainly use credit cards (38%) â based on data from digital intheround.
 
Going forward, the future of e-commerce will be dominated by technology driving already existing trends in a much broader context. Examples abound:
 
â   artificial intelligence (AI), Augmented Reality (AR) and 3D assistants driving personalization to new avenues (Amazonâs Room Decorator tool provides a glimpse to the future)
 
â    Metaverse potentially revolutionizing shopping by means of introducing new meta markets
 
â    The internet of things  driving customer retention and efficiency gains
 
As e-commerce matures and grows, it will be making its way to an experience-first, immersive next-gen phase, bringing a (welcome) shift of power toward the consumer and inevitably a drastic shake-up of the value chain as a result of increased competition.

Central Bank Digital Currency
 
The European Central Bank (ECB) has released a paper on digital economy, privacy and central bank digital currency (CBDC).
 
The ongoing digitalization of the economy has profound implications for the way payments are settled with declining use of cash, increased online transactions using electronic or digital payments and privately issued digital currencies.
 
These developments have led to a debate among policy makers about the potential creation of CBDC.
 
Electronic payments create vast amounts of data, so data privacy is one motivation for such a change to our monetary system. While more data can in principle enable better products and services, economists have become increasingly concerned about the anti‐ competitive effects of large data monopolies in the form of dominant digital platforms.
 
Broadly speaking, digital public money in the form of a CBDC may have a comparative advantage at providing privacy because, unlike private sector alternatives, it is not subject to profit‐maximization incentives.
 
This paper speaks to this debate by developing a model of financial intermediation, payment choice, and privacy in the digital economy.
 
Cash preserves anonymity but cannot be used for more efficient online transactions. By contrast, bank deposits can be used online but do not preserve anonymity. Banks use the information contained in deposit flows to charge merchants in need of financing. Payment tokens issued by digital platforms allow merchants to hide information from payment flows from banks but enable platforms to stifle competition by keeping merchants locked in a “walled garden”.
 
An independent digital payment instrument such as a CBDC that allows agents to share their payment data with selected parties can overcome all frictions and achieves the efficient allocation. It also prevents anti‐competitive practices by platforms, further raising efficiency. These results have important policy implications for ECB.

I talk a lot about #Auditchain and am asked "What problem does it solve"? There are so many companies with errors on their financial reports, be it by fraud or negligence. #XBRL is the open international standard for digital business reporting, improving reporting in the public interest. By using XBRL to produce and display reports... instead of PDFs, Excel or html, as we do now... we make accounting information #machinereadable and more useful. Machine readable information is becoming more important. Auditchain makes it super reliable and puts in on a blockchain that cannot be altered or shut down. 

If a system can "read" what's in your ledger against what's on your machine readable report, it can bring to your attention the issues and noone can hide them. To prevent THIS from happening:

 The genesis of the token economy, the true open market, and the disruption of money and finance and data storage that lets everyone participate in a global system that is exponentially more secure and democratic. Bitcoin started it and is here to stay.

YOUR IDEAL CRYPTO CLIENT?

I just got off a thought provoking call with David Bocca, group member who, like me, is letting go some clients to give himself some breathing space to learn, and consider new accounting practice models. That got us to thinking about the kinds of crypto clients we want to work with. 

It's an instant no to any that don't want to pay tax and take advice. So which crypto clients DO we want? We should know before they turn up, right?

The trend is to move away from individuals, and target tech -friendly micro businesses and SME's. Scaled services combining the regular reporting with virtual CFO type work and year-round compliance. I think of this as business management services.

Although individuals increasingly need help with crypto...

In my practice, scaled down to 3 employees and 20 clients, we lead with the bookkeeping so we can do it right with tech, and manage the rest in our workflow. Fees average around $2K a month, but going for higher. 

We're getting ready in 2022, I guess, for the crypto and Web3 businesses that will need us in 2023-24. For $4K+ a month with bookkeeping.

In the meantime, would we do ad-hoc crypto consulting..? I don't know yet. 
Your thoughts?

Electra Frost created a private event for ACCOUNTANTS ON-CHAIN - Crypto & Blockchain Accountants and Advisers Group.

A private group for accountants, bookkeepers and advisers to support each other in keeping pace with rapid developments in bitcoin, crypto and blockchain tech adoption. 

A safe place to ask questions and share ideas - whether it's about dealing with client crypto matters, how blockchain innovation affects our future professional practices, what the ATO should do, how we can best service the growing numbers of investors and SMEs in this space, and more. 

We closely follow regulatory, accounting standards and tax reforms that have been put in motion, new software to make our work easier, and industry events, quality education and CPD. 

This group was set up by Electra Frost (tax agent, FIPA, CTA) and Gordon Christian, co-founders of Digital Playhouse - a social enterprise and registered charity in Central Coastal Qld - on a mission to accelerate digital technology and blockchain learning and adapt skills to emerging opportunities. We believe that crypto-literate accounting professionals are integral to guiding compliant and successful crypto/Web3 businesses for the benefit of the Australian economy.

#braggreport Last October, the Select Committee on Australia as a Technology and Financial Centre chaired by Senator Andrew Bragg made recommendations in the **[Bragg report](https://www.aph.gov.au/Parliamentary_Business/Committees/Senate/Financial_Technology_and_Regulatory_Technology/AusTechFinCentre/Final_report)**, which, if adopted, would reflect a radical departure from the current regulatory environment for cryptocurrency and other digital assets. 

Of the 12 Final Report recommendations, 11 were either noted or accepted by Government. DAOs represent the most significant erosion to the tax base and are being prioritised, and the Board of Taxation terms of reference for their upcoming review of digital assets and digital asset transactions will cover nearly every aspect of tax law - issues from interpretation of existing law and areas for new tax policy. 

There is no likelihood of tax clarity this year, as the BoT report is not due until Dec 2022. So it's unlikely that any crypto tax policy will find its way into the upcoming budget or election platform as the work wonât be complete.

This was Senator Bragg's address to Blockchain Week 2022 on March 21:
https://www.startupdaily.net/2022/03/senator-andrew-bragg-calls-for-a-single-act-for-the-australian-government-to-regulate-crypto-and-blockchain/

JOIN HIS DISCORD CHANNEL - it's a great place to watch him and other crypto tax-pros in action answering curly questions! https://discord.gg/KAeK9QATcw

DAOs are like the vehicles for De-Fi, in a blockchain based business model and structure, that can have any number of members located in various unknown jurisdictions. It's a moving feast and being wholly compliant really isn't possible. 

Hopefully, Recommendation No. 4 of the Bragg Report (The Government establish a new Decentralised Autonomous Organisation company structure.) will be acted on soon. I think we need a consistent global approach, because updating DTAs will take forever 

DAOs operate âon decentralised blockchain infrastructure, whose operations are pre-determined in open source code and enforced through smart contractsâ. This means they donât fit neatly within Australiaâs existing company structures. The committee suggests that the resultant regulatory uncertainty âis preventing the establishment of projects of significant scale in Australiaâ. It takes the view that implementing a DAO company structure will âdrive economic activity in this space and be a magnet for Australian innovation for DAOs, driving local jobs and tax revenueâ.

While so much of the current tech innovation is experimental, it's a good time to develop our understanding of blockchain concepts with sessions like this. So we can contribute our experience to real projects in future.  #blockchaineducation #futureofbusiness

As a creative industries accountant since 2000, there's no question about it, I have to ride this wave! Creatives like both money and social goodness, I've experienced. They especially like finding ways to make NEW money to keep doing good... sweet passive income... now we're talking crypto. Seeing huge opportunities for my clients and friends, it's such a blast learning about Australian blockchain innovations. It pushed me to get qualified with Blockchain Collective in 2022, so we can formulate better prospects for creative businesses this decade.

Check out the Creative Industries Faculty at QUT (Queensland University of Technology) (article below) - they developed a world-class blockchain prototype system for digital rights management and royalty distribution, opening the door for small publishers to new commercial opportunities. Micropayments are triggered via smart contracts to all creative professionals involved in the writing and publishing process, with all purchases tracked with a custom digital ledger. Brisbane micropublisher Sue Wright's project, Tiny Owl Workshop, is even creating 'extra' intellectual property using this technology! (love the grassroots initiatives)

Here's the thing -
Involving such new, very technical and complex processes, you see that all collaborating specialists - from IT, coding, legal, accounting, business advisory and the creatives themselves - we all need to learn about applied blockchain, game theory and tokenomics. To conceive and build out these beautiful automated systems that livelihoods will depend on, we begin by understanding the main architecture for structuring a sustainable distributed economy.

Seems daunting but it's OK, we're learning from each other because it all really is so new and coming at a crazy pace. One way is *learning by doing*, even just a little bit at a time... as early adopters I think we can influence how future applications will create and distribute economic value in line with our human values. 

The blockchain community is full of inspiring people who see a better world ahead, creative thinkers, welcoming and encouraging, some have been very kind to me. We pay it forward.

My door is open to curious colleagues :) 

I was just sharing this article, and felt more to say, so excited about 2022!

El Salvador could not have made BTC legal tender without the Lightning Network (network 2) scalability.

"The Lightning Network is a decentralized scaling solution for Bitcoin that abstracts small transactions between users away from the Bitcoin blockchain. It utilizes smart contracts to settle the final balance of those payments into the Bitcoin âbase layerâ while ensuring that all transactions abide by the rules of the Bitcoin protocol.

Lightning payments are thus not recorded on the Bitcoin blockchain â only transactions that either open or close a channel are. By not requiring the transaction fees or wait times that on-chain Bitcoin transactions typically do, Lightning enables its users to enjoy affordable, fast and private payments."

https://www.nasdaq.com/articles/lightning-network-capacity-breaks-1500-btc-2021-06-17

Financial literacy in the digital age begins with being able to distinguish between centralised and decentralised purpose.

This article is from the standpoint of centralised authority on issues of identity and data in cashless transacting with individuals. The innovations in this area are being driven by blockchain (Permissionless, Trustless, Decentralised Identity Systems) but the Centralised Authorities are not pursuing public and control-less systems of self-governance. Theirs is a different blockchain use case for digital identity, data and monetary capture.

Be alert to double-speak when hearing about "protections" to the end-user when the real intention is to control and profit from the end-user.

Will the Australian Commissioner of Taxation now update its view (extracted below) that BTC is not a foreign currency? The basis for that view no longer holds now that El Salvador has adopted BTC as legal tender.

TD 2014/25 at [33] "As bitcoin is not a monetary unit recognised and adopted by the laws of any other sovereign State as the means for discharging monetary obligations for all transactions and payments in a sovereign State, it is not 'foreign currency' for the purposes of Division 775 of the ITAA 1997."

Do you know about the School of Arts movement of the 19th century? In the spirit of enlightenment these grand structures were built in our towns, for public lectures and diffusion of knowledge and skills. Out of these humble voluntary operations developed the local public library, the modern neighbourhood centre, and formal systems of adult and technical education.

"Underpinning the movement were the ideas that industry and society would benefit from a scientifically educated artisan class and that a new breed of inventors would arise from this class."

Now, in this digital age of converging technologies and the Fourth Industrial Revolution, transforming all our jobs and business models, should we repeat history with "Schools of the Future"? 
 

Pictured: Bundaberg School of Arts, 1888

It needs to be known that many accountants are really not coping with the unpredictable nature of the current situation and their massively increased burden in the last 5 weeks, ongoing. With so many distressed business owners relying on them.. waiting for government stimulus, and it keeps looking harder and harder to get. 

Some accountants have been going non-stop 7 days p.w since the disruption, trying to keep up with all this plus their usual workload and family needs... it’s been unrelenting. 

Take a day off, you say? It’s not possible to take a day off when there’s so much to keep up with every week, constant changes in legislation, rules and ATO guides ... that still don’t answer the clients’ questions. We must keep up every time there’s a whole lot of new info, every week/day/hour because if we give wrong advice then we may be responsible for mistakes and misinterpretations. This really isn’t a time we want to cause a client in difficulty to miss out on much needed money, or cause an ATO audit of their JobKeeper entitlement after they spent it or... heaven forbid... be liable under the Crimes Act for a PAYG withholding irregularity increasing their CashFlow Boost.

I’m fine, thanks for asking, just very troubled & sad for my industry peers who‘ve felt pushed too far

I am SO PROUD of my visionary brother who is launching this one of a kind school in the Upper Hunter. 🤩 The Australian music industry’s next generation of shining stars will be found in the big skies of regional Australia. ♥️

"Each day will start with the whole school singing and some form of physical activity, whether that's something like dance or exercise, to start the brain and the creative process," he said. 

"As they get older they will get access to instrumental tuition and the opportunity to participate in ensembles. 

"As teaching staff we'll be finding ways for music to permeate through the other KLAs. Every day will have music in it... we're looking at an hour a day." 

Mr English said he expected the school to grow over the next decade to one class per grade from kindergarten to year 12, with capacity for around 400 students.

He said students who join the school from year three will audition.

AFTRS: Lights! Camera! Accounting! Has anyone ever said "behind every great film is a great accountant"? Well, films don't happen without them... and with a shortage of production accountants in Australia I had to do this Australian Film Television and Radio School course to find out more. I'm so glad I did!

Thanks to Jane Corden and Jill Hewitt from Moneypenny Services for so clearly and insightfully explaining all of the tasks and requirements of a Production Accountant and how to manage a production budget from lock-off stage to completion. You really pack a lot of high quality content into a short course! It was great to be brought up to date on the relevant awards, tax offsets, QAPE and audit requirements too.

I'd recommend the 12-week industry certificate course starting in February. Australia's film industry needs more production accountants, and this is the ticket for anyone with a bookkeeping or accounting background who wants to explore an exciting new career path.
